questions.description: Barry, Denise and Ciara are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Plush Interiors Limited (the “Company”). The C…
questions.text:
Barry, Denise and Ciara are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Plush Interiors Limited (the “Company”). The Company was established by their late mother Lulu and each of them acquired their shares under Lulu’s will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are Barry and Denise who also work in the Company. Ciara left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in New York and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, Barry and Denise declare a dividend which the Company pays to Ciara, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Ciara came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from Barry and Denise whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because neither had cash on hand to buy her shares. 
Barry and Denise come to you for advice when Ciara contacted them again and announced that she has sold her shares in the Company to Philip, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later Barry and Denise were approached by Philip who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Ciara and asked them to register him as a member. Barry and Denise are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company’s constitution, you see section 95(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014 applies and that the directors of the company may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register the transfer of any shares. You are required to advise Barry and Denise on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Philip. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: June and Harry are the only directors and equal shareholders in Outdoor Craic Limited (the “Company”) which operates an …
questions.text:
June and Harry are the only directors and equal shareholders in Outdoor Craic Limited (the “Company”) which operates an outdoor adventure business based in the west of Ireland. The Company owns approximately 50 acres of mountainous terrain on which it operates
its business. An opportunity has arisen to acquire a neighbouring mountain from a company called The Brothers’ Reserve Ltd which is owned 50:50 by Harry and his brother Bart which has agreed to sell the mountain to the Company for €400,000 or lease the mountain to the Company for 20 years at a market rent of €15,000 per annum. You are required to advise both the Company and The Brothers’ Reserve Ltd on their respective obligations under (and only under the following sections): section 238 of the Companies Act 2014; and section 239 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “When proceedings are brought against the director of an insolvent company under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, …
questions.text: “When proceedings are brought against the director of an insolvent company under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, case law indicates that unless the director can show that he or she acted honestly and responsibly in relation to the conduct of the affairs of the company, the Court will make a restriction order”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Global Diverse Holdings Limited (“Holco”) is an Irish registered company which has its headquarters in Limerick. It does…
questions.text: Global Diverse Holdings Limited (“Holco”) is an Irish registered company which has its headquarters in Limerick. It does not itself trade, but it has a number of wholly-owned subsidiaries which do trade and which distribute the profit they make, up to Holco. Malawi Textiles Limited is one of its subsidiaries (“Subco”) and it is also an Irish registered company. Subco operates in Malawi where it benefits from cheap local labour to dye clothing for multiple retailers worldwide. The directors of Subco are the same as the directors of Holco. Subco’s board of directors meets in Ireland and directs local management at the factory in Malawi through the CEO who is native to Malawi and who lives close to the factory and manages it on a day to day basis. Last year there was a chemical leakage at Subco’s factory in Malawi and as a result of this, a local river was severely polluted. Shauna is a lawyer representing a number of local families whose fishing livelihood was ruined on account of the leak. Shauna approaches you for advice as to whether the families have a cause of action against Holco. The reason why she is interested in suing Holco as well as Subco is because Subco recently distributed its profits up to Holco and has little cash reserves and the property from which Subco operates its factory premises in Malawi is in fact owned by Holco. You are required to advise Shauna on the law relating to disregarding separate legal personality in Ireland and on her chances of her clients’ success if they are to sue Holco. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: Fintan is sole director and shareholder in Fintan's Freakish Fish Limited (the "Company") which operates a pet shop in Naul specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company’s business had been going downhill and Fintan, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: Fintan removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company's premises and purported to sell it to his wife Joan who has her own pet shop in Lexlip, although it does not appear that Joan paid for the stock; Fintan caused the Company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Benny. Last month the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to him under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to the two dispositions identified at (a) and (b). In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “The members of a company can give their sanction to a proposed course of action in four distinct ways: By a resolution …
questions.text: “The members of a company can give their sanction to a proposed course of action in four distinct ways: By a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held (whether physically or virtually); By a unanimous written resolution or in the case of a single member company, by the written decision of the sole member; By a majority written resolution; and By an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement.” You are required to explain the operation of each of these four means and you must cite all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Brian is a director of Alpine Cars Limited (the “Company”) which operates a car sales business. Brian is not a sharehold…
questions.text: Brian is a director of Alpine Cars Limited (the “Company”) which operates a car sales business. Brian is not a shareholder in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by George although Brian has worked in the Company for 10 years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. There are three other directors (all family members, making four directors in total). George, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Brian has been “double-jobbing” for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Helen. Specifically, George has heard that: Helen has been buying cars from Brian that were traded in to the Company at a discounted price before they sell them on for a significant profit on their website; and Brian has been using the Company’s garage-workshop to service the cars late at night and on Sundays when the garage-workshop is closed. He has used spare parts belonging to the Company but has not reimbursed the Company for these. You are required to advise George on the following: whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Brian, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; and the remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breach of duties. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.text: Toby, Rocco and Maya are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Doggo Enterprises Limited (the “Company”). The Company was established by their late mother Madge and each of them acquired their shares under Madge’s will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are Toby and Maya who also work in the Company. Rocco left the country a number of years ago and lives with his husband in France and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because neither had cash on hand to buy his shares. Toby and Maya come to you for advice after Rocco contacted them again and announced that he has sold his shares in the Company to Brizzo, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later Toby and Maya were approached by Brizzo who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Rocco and asked them to register him as a member. Toby and Maya are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company’s constitution, you see section 95(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014 applies and that the directors of the company may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register the transfer of any shares. questions.description: Shelia is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) to be receiver to I Woke up…
questions.text: Shelia is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) to be receiver to I Woke up with Coﬀee Limited (the “Company”) on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company’s principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in Balbriggan where it mixes, packages and distributes coﬀee beans sourced from a fair trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for €3,800,000 in 2015. Shelia believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €3,300,000 and has a firm oﬀer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Shelia that he might be prepared to oﬀer €3,700,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Shelia is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Murph, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company asking Shelia to make sure she gets the best price for the property. Shelia is aware that Murph gave a personal guarantee to the Bank and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing to the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Shelia on her duties as a receiver having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company and whether she owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer, you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of DRK Limited (the “Company”), the other director of which is h…
questions.text: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of DRK Limited (the “Company”), the other director of which is his wife, Deirdre. The Company operated the business of travel agent for a number of years. The Company has loan facilities from Big Bank plc which are secured by a floating charge over the Company’s assets, a fixed charge over its leasehold interest in the premise it rents and the personal guarantees of its directors, Damien and Deirdre. A number of customers were owed money by the Company arising from a mix up in holidays and one, Colin, was owed €5,000 and was constantly calling to Damien’s and Deirdre’s family home seeking payment. In the last few weeks, the Revenue Commissioners petitioned to have the company wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts and the High Court granted that petition, appointing Jack Harmer the liquidator. You are retained by Jack to advise as to the law relating to the following payments by the Company: Some three months before the petition was presented, Colin called at the directors’ home seeking payment. Damien was out that evening and Deirdre, who was concerned at Colin’s ever-threatening manner, wrote him a cheque for €5,000 in respect of refund owed to him; (8 marks) The day before the petition was heard by the High Court, Damien transferred €30,000 from one account the Company had into its overdrawn current account which both Damien and Deirdre had guaranteed; (8 marks) The day after the petition was presented, the Company’s bank debited its current account for €10,000 in respect of accrued interest on the Company’s loans; (4 marks) You are required to advise Jack Harmer the liquidator on the law in relation to each of the three payments, distinguishing (a), (b) and (c). In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods from wood for the tourist market. He employs six people, and his business has been very successful. Sean comes to you for advice and during the course of the consultation he tells you a number of things about his business. He is considering incorporating the business but is not sure of the advantages and disadvantages of doing this. He is about to acquire a new factory outlet, whereby the products will be made in the same location as they are sold and to where members of the public can come and see him and his employees at work. Sean will need to borrow from his bank to acquire the new premises. Recently, one of his employees received a nasty cut from a chisel which severed an artery and caused him to be off work for two months and although Sean’s insurance company paid out the claim, Sean had a few sleepless nights worrying over the claim until he received their letter confirming it was covered in full by the policy. Sean's longest serving employee, Fred, has for some time been pressing Sean to form a partnership with him so he can share in the success of the business. While Sean is agreeable to giving Fred a slice of the action, he is uncomfortable with forming a partnership with Fred. While Sean has a bookkeeper, he has never bothered having his accounts audited. You are required to advise Sean on the advantages and disadvantages of incorporating his business as a private company limited by shares under Part 2 of the Companies Act 2014, having particular regard to the matters raised by Sean during the consultation. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statue law.

questions.description: The liquidator of Timber products Limited (the “Company) which was incorporated under the Companies Act 2014 contacts yo…
questions.text: The liquidator of Timber products Limited (the “Company) which was incorporated under the Companies Act 2014 contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has just been appointed to the Company by the High Court on the petition of the Revenue Commissioners and needs your help in unravelling the Company’s affairs and determining the order of priorities. Specifically, you are advised that: A company called Raw Materials Limited has a retention of title clause in its contract for the sale of three batches of timber it supplied to the Company worth €75,000. The liquidator advises you that there is only one batch of timber untouched as one was made into furniture and remains on the Company’s premises whereas the third batch was made into furniture and sold. The clause in question provided “the property in the timber shall not transfer to the Company until all sums owing to the Vendor have been paid. In addition, until the timber has been paid for, any goods made from the timber shall become the property of the Vendor. Furthermore, where the timber has been used to make goods and is sold, the Vendor is entitled to the proceeds of the sale of the goods made from the timber which shall be held in trust by the Company for the Vendor”. The liquidator wants to know whether he must return the timber, the furniture made therefrom and the proceeds of sale of the goods sold, payment for which has just been made to the liquidator. A company called brass Fittings Ltd supplied hinges and such fittings to the Company. It obtained security for the goods it supplied to the Company in the form of a fixed charge over the Company’s book debts and a charge over the bank account into which the book debts were to be paid. The charge over the book debts was registered and the liquidator can see that book debts were paid into a restricted bank account as required. The liquidator sees that the charge over the book debts was registered under section 409; however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was not registered under section 409 and the liquidator tells you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of the sum due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator in respect of each of the foregoing matters. Your answer should clearly distinguish the two questions asked in (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs an estate agency. They are also shareho…
questions.text: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the Company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John’s solicitor’s practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to john. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agency. In recent times, however, John’s recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than john to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below the market rent, he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the best interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John’s attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked john to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise john as to the following matters: Whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014; AND Whether your answer would be different if the Company’s constitution or a shareholders’ agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders’’ businesses by referring clients to them. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law. You must distinguish your answers to parts (a) and (b) of the question, both of which carry equal marks.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line wine shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Comp…
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line wine shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the Bank can freeze at its discretion. Both charges were created in 2016. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which then appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation. Following his appointment, the Company’s liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under section 409 of the Companies Act 2014; and the charge over the book debts was registered under section 409; however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was not registered under section 409 and the liquidator tells you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of the sum due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) which must be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Irish Diverse Holdings Limited (“Holco”) is an Irish registered company which has its headquarters in Cork. It does not …
questions.text: Irish Diverse Holdings Limited (“Holco”) is an Irish registered company which has its headquarters in Cork. It does not itself trade but has a number of wholly-owned subsidiaries which do trade and which distribute the profit they make up to Holco. Bangladesh Cottons Limited is one of its subsidiaries (Subco) and it is also an Irish registered company. Subco operates in Bangladesh where it benefits from cheap local labour to dye clothing for multiple retailers worldwide. The directors of Subco are the same as the directors of Holco. Subco’s board of directors meets in Ireland and directs local management at the factory in Bangladesh through the CEO who is native to Bangladesh and who lives close to the factory and manages it on a day-to-day basis. Last year there was a chemical leakage at Subco’s factory in Bangladesh and as a result of this, a local river was severely polluted. Shelia, who is a lawyer representing a number of local families whose fishing livelihood was ruined on account of the leak, approaches you for advice as to whether the families have a cause of action against Holco. The reason why she is interested in suing Holco as well as Subco is because Subco recently distributed its profits up to Holco and has little cash reserves and the property from which Subco operates its factory in Bangladesh is in fact owned by Holco. You are required to advise Shelia on the law relating to disregarding separate legal personality in Ireland and on the chances of her clients’ success in Irish company law if they are to sue Holco in the Irish courts. You must support your answer with relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: “The members of a company can give their sanction to a proposed course of action in four distinct ways: by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held (whether physically or virtually); by a unanimous written resolution or in the case of a single member company, by the written decision of the sole member; by a majority written resolution; and by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement.” You are required to explain the operation of each of these four means and you must support your answer with references to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

questions.text: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie's Freakish Fish Limited (the "Company") which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company’s business had been going downhill, so Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: he removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company's premises and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare, although it does not appear that Freda paid for the stock; he caused the Company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to her under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to the two dispositions identified at (a) and (b). Advise the liquidator. In support of your answer, you are required to cite relevant case law and statutory provisions. There are 10 marks available for answering (a) and 10 marks available for answering (b).

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the “Company”) which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul …
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the “Company”) which operates a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company, which is owned by a family consortium headed-up by Simon, although Paul has worked in the Company for 10 years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. There are three other directors, in addition to Paul, in the Company (all family members, making four directors in total). Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been “double-jobbing” for the last two years, selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Specifically, Simon has heard that Jackie has been buying second-hand trade-in cars from the Company through Paul at a discounted price before selling on those cars through the website at a considerable profit to herself and Paul. Simon has also learned that Paul has been using the Company’s garage-workshop to service those cars late at night and on Sundays when the garage-workshop is closed, using spare parts belonging to the Company, but without reimbursing the Company for those parts. You are required to advise Simon on the following: whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Paul, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; and the remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breach of duties. In answering you must cite relevant case law and statutory provisions and apply them to the facts provided above.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “Company”). The Co…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “Company”). The Company was established by their late mother Madge and each of them acquired their shares under Madge’s will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because neither had cash on hand to buy her shares. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she has sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy and asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company’s constitution, you see section 95(1)(a) of the Companies Act 2014 applies and that the directors of the company may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register the transfer of any shares. You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. You are required to cite relevant case and statutory provisions to support your answer.

questions.description: Jackie's business has failed. For the last 6 years, Jackie operated a flower shop from a unit in a shopping center outsi…
questions.text: Jackie's business has failed. For the last 6 years, Jackie operated a flower shop from a unit in a shopping center outside of Dublin. The business was conducted through her company, which is now called Smell that DAC (the “Company”) of which Jackie was the sole shareholder, and one of two directors, the other director being Jackie's former fiancé, Ted. Jackie contacted Ted to tell him the company was insolvent, and they agreed to hold a directors’ meeting with a view to putting the company into creditors’ voluntary winding up. On meeting with Jackie and Ted, you elicit the following information: The company now owes €500,000 in back rent and taxes. The company has struggled through the recession, as people have had less disposable income to spend on Flowers. The final straw has been an increase in rent on the shop unit. The company rents, which was increased from €20,000 to €40,000 per month. The Revenue Commissioners have not been paid VAT on sales or PRSI or PAYE due on Jackie’s salary from the Company for three months. In addition, the revenue Commissioners have issued an assessment for €50,000 in relation to back tax owed to them, which the company cannot afford to pay. The circumstances in which this has become due were that Jackie had hired a teenage girl to work a few hours on a Saturday morning, paying her cash in hand. When this was discovered by the Revenue Commissioners, they claimed for lost PRSI and PAYE tax on the girl's earnings over a two-year period. The Company's books and records have been maintained by a local bookkeeper to an acceptable standard, however, on the way to delivering the books and records to the bookkeeper for last 12-month period, Jackie's car was stolen, and the books and records destroyed when joy-rider thieves of set fire to the car. Ted, the Company’s second director, has not been active in the business of the Company. He agreed to be a director whilst engaged to Jackie because every company needs two directors, but he has not been active in the company's business apart from signing accounts and forms when asked to do so. Although they since split off, the breakup was amicable but Ted had only agreed to stay on until Jackie could find someone else to become a director. The liquidator has been looking to meet with them both but to date, while Jackie has met with him, Ted has not, having left a message with the liquidator’s office to say “there is nothing I can help you with”. You are required to advise both Jackie and Ted on the likelihood that they will be restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, and in particular on the defences available to them. Support your answer with appropriate reference to relevant case law and statutory provisions.

He has also used spare parts belonging to the Company but has not reimbursed the Company for these. You are required to advise Simon on the following: Whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Paul, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; and The remedies that are available to the company arising from any breach of duties. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line wine shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Comp…
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line wine shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the bank can freeze at its discretion. Both charges were created in 2016. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which then appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation. Following her appointment, the Company's liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: Through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favor of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under Section 409 of the Companies Act 2014; and The charge over the book debts was registered under Section 409; however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was not registered under Section 409 and the liquidator told you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of sum due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) which must be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks. questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the “Company”) on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from fair-trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for 2,500,000 in 2017. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1,500,000 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the Managing Director and sole shareholder in the company, asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the bank and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount or into the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. questions.text: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs in an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each holds 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares are being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to just to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favorable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency years John solicitor’s practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue for John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as a seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last six months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only five referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic down turn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty claiming that in ceasing to maintain his levels of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder, and he pointed to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and they had never asked for a lower rent, and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that this that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals, to him a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise John as to the following matters: whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 (15 marks) And whether your answer will be different if the constitution or a shareholders’ agreement contained the provision that the Company would support shareholders’ businesses by referring clients to them. (5 marks) You are required to answer both part (a) (which carries 15 marks) and part (b) (which carries 5 marks). In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: William’s World of Colors Limited (the “Company”) is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing…
questions.text:
William’s World of Colors Limited (the “Company”) is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing paint to the Irish construction industry. For the past number of years, business has been booming. Two of the Company's customers have had lucrative contracts for the painting of new housing estates. The Company has three directors -William, Peggy and Eugene- and is a single-member private limited company, all shares being owned by William. William is the son of the Company's founder Ann is a managing director. Peggy, his daughter, is the Sales Director and Eugene is her husband. Eugene is a non-executive director who works as a local self-employed solicitor. His involvement with the Company is very limited and he never attends meetings of the directors. In recent times the Company's fortunes have faltered. Both of its major customers have gone into liquidation, owing the Company several hundred thousand euro. Both customers were always slow payers, but the Company received nothing from them for months although it continued to supply them with paint. William realised things were bad when the Company's bank manager telephoned him to say that the Company had exceeded its overdraft. William persuaded the bank to extend the overdraft to pay wages and give him time to collect some outstanding debts. The Company chugged along for another month but then William realised the Company was insolvent and without any hope of immediate improvement. At that point William place the company in the creditor’s voluntary liquidation. The Company’s liquidator discovered that the Company's record keeping was sporadic and that management accounts had not been prepared with the frequency that would have alerted the Company's directors to the fact it's two main customers had not been discharging what was owed by them for some time. Moreover, it seems that the Company had not been paying the Revenue Commissioners the PAYE and PRSI it had collected from its employees and owed the Revenue some €300,000.
You are required to advise on the likelihood that each of the three directors will be made the subject of a restriction order pursuant to section 819 of the companies Act 2014 and on the defenses available to the directors. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

You are required to advise Bob on: How the Company could have been struck off the register and how it can be restored to the register; and What are the legal consequences of the Company being struck off the register and of the Company being restored to the register. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) to be receiver to Totally PC…
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the “Company”) on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings.  The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company’s principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair-trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for €2,500,000 in 2007. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1,500,000 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company, asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing to the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver, having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company, and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In you answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Referring to the principle that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members Lardner J stated “the statutory ru…
questions.text: Referring to the principle that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members Lardner J stated “the statutory rules of company law in which the principle is implicit apply to the relationship between holding companies and subsidiaries and to transactions between them and third parties”. You are required to discuss this application of the principle of separate corporate personality in the specific context of holding companies and subsidiaries, citing all relevant case and stature law in your answer.

questions.description: “It is now well recognised that a company’s members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three way…
questions.text: “It is now well recognised that a company’s members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous or majority written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Jackie’s business has failed.  For the last 6 years Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a shopping centre outs…
questions.text: Jackie’s business has failed.  For the last 6 years Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a shopping centre outside of Dublin.  The business was conducted through Smell That DAC (the “Company”) of which Jackie was the sole shareholder and one of two directors, the other director being Jackie’s former fiancé, Ted.  Jackie contacted Ted to tell him the company was insolvent, and they agreed to hold a directors’ meeting with a view to putting the Company into creditors’ voluntary winding up.  On subsequently meeting with Jackie and Ted, you elicit the following information: The Company now owed €500,000 in back rent and taxes.  The Company has struggled through the recession, as people have had less disposable income to spend on flowers.  The final straw has been an increase in rent in relation to the shop unit that the Company rents, as it was increased from €20,000 to €40,000 per month. The Revenue Commissioners have not been paid VAT on sales, or the PRSI or PAYE due on Jackie’s salary from the Company from three months. In addition, the Revenue Commissioners have issued an assessment from €50,000 in relation to back tax owed to it, which the Company cannot afford to pay.  The circumstances in which this has become due were that Jackie had hired a teenage girl to work a few hours on a Saturday morning, paying her cash in hand.  When this was discovered by the Revenue Commissioners, they claimed for lost PRSI and PAYE tax on girl’s earnings over a two-year period. The Company’s books and records have been maintained by a local book-keeper to an acceptable standard, however, on the way to delivering the books and records to the book-keeper for the last 12 month period, Jackie’s car was stolen and the books and records destroyed after the joy-rider thieves set fire to the car. Ted, the Company’s second director, has not been active in the business of the Company.  He says he agreed to be a director whilst engaged to Jackie because every company needs two directors, but he has not been active in the Company’s business, only signing accounts and forms when asked to do so.  Although they have since split up, it was amicable, and Ted only agreed to stay on until Jackie could find someone else to become a director. The liquidator has been looking to meet with them both, but to date only Jackie has met with him.  Ted left a message with the liquidator’s office to say “there is nothing I can help you with”. You are required to advise both Jackie and Ted on the likelihood that they will be restricted under section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 and in particular on the defences available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareh…
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitors practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic down turn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise on the following matters: whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014; and whether your answer would be different if the Company's constitution or a shareholder’s agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them. You are required to answer both part (a) (which carries 15 marks) and part (b) (which carries 5 marks).  In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as t…
questions.text: “Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance.” You are required to discuss this statement, and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and stature law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line book shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Comp…
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line book shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the Bank can freeze at its discretion. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which than appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation.  Following his appointment, the Company’s liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver, was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under section 409 of the Companies Act 2014; and while the charge over the book debts was registered under section 409, however, the charge created over a bank account into which book debts was paid was not registered under section 409.  The liquidator also tells you that the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the due to them ahead of sums due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have r…
questions.text: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have regard to the interests of other persons; however, all such duties are owed to the company.” You are required to discuss and explain this statement.  In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), establ…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their late mother Madge. They received their shares from Madge under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because neither had cash on hand to buy her shares. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's constitution you see that the optional provision set out in section 95(1)(a) of the Companies act 2014 applies and that the directors may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register any transfer of any share.

questions.description: Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of Ultra is no longer of central importance to questions…
questions.text: Following the enactment of the Companies Act 2014, the doctrine of Ultra is no longer of central importance to questions of corporate capacity.’ You are required to discuss and explain this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Emma and David are the directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the ‘Company’), which operates an outdoor …
questions.text: Emma and David are the directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the ‘Company’), which operates an outdoor adventure business based in the west of Ireland. The company owns approximately 50 acres of mountainous terrain on which it operates its business. An opportunity has arisen to acquire a neighbouring mountain from Mountain Men Ltd, a company which is owned 50:50 by David and his brother Bart. Mountain Men Ltd has agreed to sell the mountain to the Company (Fresh Air Limited) for €400,000 or lease the mountain to the Company for 20 years at a market rent of €15,000 per annum. You are required to advise the Company and Mountain Men Ltd on their obligations under (and only under): Section 238 of the Companies Act 2014; and Section 239 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: ‘In limited liability companies, dividends can only be declared from distributable profits, and assets can only be trans…
questions.text: ‘In limited liability companies, dividends can only be declared from distributable profits, and assets can only be transferred to shareholders at an under value when the company has sufficient distributable profits. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Sean is a wood turner who has operated as a sole trader for the last 5 years, making wooden bowls and assorted other goo…
questions.text: Sean is a wood turner who has operated as a sole trader for the last 5 years, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods from wood for the tourist market. He employs six people and his business has been very successful. Sean comes to you for advice and during the course of the consultation; he tells you a number of things about his business: He is considering incorporating the business but is not sure of the advantages and disadvantages of doing this. He is about to acquire a new factory outlet, whereby the products will be made in the same location as they are sold, and to where members of the public can come and see him and his employees at work. Sean will need to borrow from his bank to acquire the new premises. Recently, one of his employees received a nasty cut from a chisel which severed an artery, and caused him to be off work for two months and, although Sean’s insurance company paid out on the claim, Sean had a few sleepless nights worrying over the claim until he received their letter confirming it was covered in full by the policy. Sean’s longest serving employee, Fred, has for some time been pressing Sean to form a partnership with him so he can share in the success of the business. While Sean is agreeable to giving Fred a sluice of action, he is very uncomfortable with forming a partnership with Fred. Although Sean has a book-keeper, he has never bothered having his accounts audited. You are required to advise Sean on the Advantages and Disadvantages of incorporating his business as a private company limited by shares under Part 2 of the Companies Act 2014, having particular regard to the matters raised by Sean during the consultation. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: While a creditor of a company is entitled ex debito justiciae (as a matter of right) to a winding-up order, the remedy o…
questions.text: While a creditor of a company is entitled ex debito justiciae (as a matter of right) to a winding-up order, the remedy of winding up is discretionary remedy and is not available in cases of disputed debts’. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the ‘Company’) which has been in the busine…
questions.text: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the ‘Company’) which has been in the business of organising corporate events since 2005. Business was generally very strong for the last number of years, and while the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last year business has never been worse. Clients are either not hosting events or are cancelling events already planned. Richard and Frank have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on both the rent for its premises and its bank loan of €2,500,000. The bank loan is secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge of the Company’s premises, which is now worth only €1,750,000. This month it is unlikely to be able to pay the Revenue Commissioners the full amount of VAT that the Company collected from its customers, having used it to pay wages. Richard and Frank believe that, if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving, as they believe that the worst of the effects of Brexit and the uncertainties in the USA, have both stabilised. Frank has approached and old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company, offering him 25% of the Company (12½% each from him and Richard). Matters came to ahead yesterday when the Company’s bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company’s bank loan being in arrears. Richard and Frank approach you for advice on: The steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; The likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; The consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed; and The duties and powers of an examiner. You are required to advice Richard and Frank in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b), (c) and (d) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: “The causes of crystallisation of a floating charge are now well settled in Irish case law”. You are required to explain…
questions.text: “The causes of crystallisation of a floating charge are now well settled in Irish case law”. You are required to explain the legal nature of a floating charge and to discuss the above statement. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Freddie is a sole director and shareholder in Freddie’s Freakish Fish Limited (“the company”) which operates a pet shop …
questions.text: Freddie is a sole director and shareholder in Freddie’s Freakish Fish Limited (“the company”) which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company’s business had been going downhill and Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: He removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company’s premises, and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare, although it does not appear that Freda paid for the stock; He caused the Company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to him under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to the two dispositions identified at (a) and (b). Advise the liquidator. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have r…
questions.text: "While company directors have a fiduciary duty to act in the interests of their company, they also have duties to have regard to the interests of other persons". You are required to discuss and explain this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the "Bank") to be receiver to Totally PC…
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the "Bank") to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the "Company") on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2m by the Company and the Company's principal asset is a premises in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair trade plantation in Brazil. The premises was acquired by the Company for €2.5m in 2007. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1.5m and has a firm offer from an interested party for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1.9m in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company, asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank, and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing to the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver, having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company, and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's Case and section 40 of the Companies Act 2014 are effectiv…
questions.text: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's Case and section 40 of the Companies Act 2014 are effective in ensuring the enforceability of a contract that has been entered into by a company, acting by its directors" You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, a court appointed liquidator who was appointed to the Plot Gets Thicker …
questions.text: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, a court appointed liquidator who was appointed to the Plot Gets Thicker Limited (the "Company"), which had been a subcontractor in the film production business. Gilbert was appointed liquidator on foot of a petition brought by the Revenue Commissioners to have the company wound up for failure to pay its debts. It appears that the Company is hopelessly insolvent, having debts in excess of €15m of which some €5m is owed to the Revenue Commissioners. Following his appointment, Gilbert began to investigate the Company's affairs and the behaviour of the Company's sole-shareholder and managing director, Reynard Fox ("Mr Fox"). Gilbert tells you: The Company's books and records are in a sorry state. Record keeping has been at best sporadic, and Gilbert is finding it difficult to piece together the Company's financial history. It appears the film extras were often paid in cash and kept off the books of the Company as there are no records of people having being paid. However, other documents suggest that the Company had supplied hundreds of extras in a recent film in which it had been involved. Basic invoice keeping also appears to have been ignored and there is little or no financial documentation to support the claims which a number of creditors have lodged against the company for both goods and services allegedly supplied. The Company has also incurred massive debts with other film companies. Its modus operandi seems to have been to agree to secure the acting services of "big-name" stars for up-coming films in return for the production companies paying the Company a significant fee of between €500,000 and €1m. Gilbert has discovered that the Company was a party to no fewer than 6 such agreements with 6 different production companies and while it received some €5m from this business, it never once managed to procure the star for the film. The 6 creditors in question have complained to Gilbert that the Company and Mr Fox traded fraudulently or, at best, recklessly in carrying on this business. Gilbert has found no evidence that the Company even sought the services of the stars in question, after taking the creditors' money. You are required to advise Gilbert on the grounds for applying to the High Court to have Mr Fox made personally liable for some or all of the Company's debts and the law in relation to the facts set out in (a) and (b). In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "When proceedings are brought against the director of an insolvent company under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, …
questions.text: "When proceedings are brought against the director of an insolvent company under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014, case law indicates that unless the director can show that he or she acted honestly and responsibly in relation to the conduct of the affairs of the company, the Court will make a restriction order". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: You are approached for advice by Denis, the financial director of a medium sized company called Festive Tidings Limited …
questions.text: You are approached for advice by Denis, the financial director of a medium sized company called Festive Tidings Limited (the "Company") which distributes Christmas greeting cards. The company has three directors (of which he is one) and is a wholly-owned subsidiary of an English company called Festive Greetings (UK) Limited. The English company holds the subsidiary's only issued share which has a nominal value of €1. Denis tells you that he and his fellow directors are coming under increasing pressure to sanction a distribution to the holding company — which is in some financial difficulties in the sum of €1m. Denis is concerned about this because while the Company is solvent and would be solvent after the payment of the € 1m to the holding company, the Company has only €500,000 in available distributable profits. Notwithstanding this, Denis is concerned that the holding company may pass a resolution directing the directors of the Company to pay the €1m sought. The Company's constitution expressly adopts the "optional provisions" contained in the Companies Act 2014 so Chapter 7 of Part 3 of the Companies Act 2014 applies. You are required to advise Denis and his fellow directors on the law relating to distributions and to the rights, duties and liabilities of directors and shareholders in relation to distributions. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: "Although section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a powerful remedy for oppressed shareholders, the exceptions to…
questions.text: "Although section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 provides a powerful remedy for oppressed shareholders, the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle and the derivative action continue to be important mechanisms for holding errant directors to account". You are required to discuss this statement. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: The liquidator of Timber Products Limited (the "Company") contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has …
questions.text: The liquidator of Timber Products Limited (the "Company") contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has just been appointed to the Company by the High Court on the petition of the Revenue Commissioners and needs your help in unravelling the Company's affairs and determining the order of priorities. Specifically, you are advised that: A company called Raw Materials Limited has a retention of title clause in its contract for the sale of three batches of timber it supplied to the Company. The Liquidator advises you that there are only two batches untouched as the third was made into furniture. The clause in question provided "The property in the timber shall not transfer to the Company until all sums owing to the Vendor have been paid and until then any goods made from the timber shall be the property of the Vendor'. The Liquidator wants to know whether he must return the timber and the furniture made therefrom. Another supplier claims to have a fixed charge over one of the Company's bank accounts. On further enquiry the Liquidator was shown a copy of a deed of fixed charge over the Company's book debts which required them to be paid into a designated bank account. As far as the Liquidator can see the account was operated by the Company whose directors lodged and withdrew monies therefrom at will. The liquidator wants to know whether this charge gives the supplier priority over the Revenue Commissioners. You are required to advise the Liquidator in respect of each of the foregoing matters. Your answer should clearly distinguish the two questions asked in (a) and (b) and you must cite all  relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The decision of the House of Lords in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22 remains as relevant in Ireland today as…
questions.text: "The decision of the House of Lords in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22 remains as relevant in Ireland today as it did when it was first decided." You are required to discuss this statement, giving reasons as to whether you agree or disagree with it. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard and Sarah are directors and 50:50 shareholders in Real World Apps Limited (the "Company") which develops and sel…
questions.text:
Richard and Sarah are directors and 50:50 shareholders in Real World Apps Limited (the "Company") which develops and sells games software for use on smart phones, The Company was formed by both of them after leaving college. The Company has been doing well and generated revenue of over €15 million last year, mostly from sales of the Company's most successful app, Mad Monkeys. Richard thinks there is a better market in war games but he has never raised his desire to develop violent game apps with Sarah, whom he knows of old to be against the proliferation of violence. While both Sarah and Richard have been working on new versions of Mad Monkeys, Richard has secretly been working on a new app called Slasher Killer Combat which as the name suggests is a graphically violent game. While he worked on this in his own time at weekends, he did spend Company time working on the game. He used the Company's software to develop the game and also used the Company's contacts to sell the game through an app website. Sales of the game are currently €50,000 per week in royalties and fees. Richard set up his own company, Richard's Apps Ltd, and it is this company that is receiving the royalties. Richard never told Sarah about his personal venture and Sarah only found out about it though trade contacts. Sarah approaches you for advice. You are required to advise Sarah on the following: whether you believe there have been any breaches of the duties owed to the Company by its director, Richard, under Part 5 of the Companies Act 2014; and
(15 marks) the remedies that are available to the Company arising from any breach of duties.
(5 marks) In your answer you must distinguish between (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member.
