questions.description: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributin…
questions.text: William's World of Colours Limited (the "Company") is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing paint to the Irish construction industry. For the past number of years, business has been booming. Two of the Company's customers have had lucrative contracts for the painting of new housing estates. The Company has three directors — William, Peggy and Eugene — and is a single-member private limited company, all shares being owned by William. William is the son of the Company's founder and is the managing director. Peggy, his daughter, is the Sales Director and Eugene is her husband. Eugene is a non-executive director who works as a local self-employed solicitor. His involvement with the Company is very limited and he rarely if ever attends meetings of the directors. In recent times the Company's fortunes have faltered. Both of its major customers have gone into liquidation, owing the Company several hundred thousand euro. Both customers were always slow payers, but the Company received nothing from them for months although it continued to supply them with paint. William realised things were bad when the Company's bank manager telephoned him to say that the Company had exceeded its overdraft. William persuaded the bank to extend the overdraft to pay wages and give him time to collect some outstanding debts. The Company chugged along for another month but then William realised that the Company was insolvent and without any hope of an immediate improvement. At that point William placed the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. The Company's liquidator discovered that the Company's record keeping was sporadic, and that management accounts had not been prepared with the frequency which would have alerted the Company's directors to the fact its two main customers has not been discharging what was owed by them for some time. You are required to advise on the likelihood that each of the three directors will be made the subject of a restriction order pursuant to section 819 of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareh…
questions.text:
Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitors practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month, there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic down turn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise on the following matters: whether John has a remedy under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014; and
(15 marks) whether your answer would be different if the Company's constitution contained a provision that the Company wouId support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them.
(5 marks) Your answer must address each of the questions at (a) and (b) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.text: Corporate Systems Ltd (the "Company") is a company limited by shares to which Parts 2 to 14 of the Companies Act 2014 (the "Act") applies. You are approached for advice by Richard who recently purchased the Company's issued share capital from Jason and Janet for €3m; Jason transferred to Richard 100 "A" shares held by him, and Janet transferred to Richard 100 "B" shares held by her. After acquiring the Company's shares, Richard discovered that Jason, who had also been the sole director of the Company, had caused the Company to do the following things the week before the sale of the shares took place: An interim dividend in the amount of €200,000 was declared and paid on the "B" shares held by Janet, notwithstanding that the Company had no distributable reserves as required by section 1 17(1) of the Act; A site owned by the Company with an open market value of €200,000 was transferred to Jason for the consideration of €1 ,000. Richard seeks your advice on the foregoing matters, and asks whether the Company can set them aside and the legal basis upon which he could cause the Company to go about setting them aside. You are required to advise Richard and in your answer you must set out all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: "The introduction of the Summary Approval Procedure contained in Chapter 7 of Part 4 of the Companies Act 2014 has resulted in a unified approach to permitting companies to engage in activities that would otherwise be prohibited provided that certain safeguards are met. The key safeguards are that the members of the company must resolve in favour of the company engaging in the activity and that the directors must make a declaration of solvency." You are required to discuss this statement, having particular regard to the rationale for the two safeguards referred to in the statement. In your answer you must cite ail relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the "Company") which has been in the busine…
questions.text: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the "Company") which has been in the business of organising corporate events since 2005. Business was very strong for the last number of years and while the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last year business has never been worse. Clients are either not hosting events or are cancelling events already planned. Richard and Frank have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on both the rent for its premises and its bank loan of some €2,500,000. The bank loan is secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge on the Company's premises, which is now worth only €1, 750,000. This month it is unlikely to be able to pay to the Revenue Commissioners the full amount of VAT it collected. Richard and Frank believe that if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving as they believe the worst of the recession is over. Frank has approached an old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company, offering him 25% of the Company (12 1h% each from him and Richard). Matters came to a head yesterday when the Company's bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company's bank loan being in arrears. Richard and Frank approach you for advice on: the steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company under Part 10 of the Companies Act 2014; the likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the (c) the consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed. You are required to advise Richard and Frank in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b) and (c) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Delia's Dahlias Limited (the "Company") has operated a successful florist shop in Limerick city for the last thirty year…
questions.text: Delia's Dahlias Limited (the "Company") has operated a successful florist shop in Limerick city for the last thirty years. The Company was founded by Delia but last year Delia retired from the business and transferred her shares in the Company to her daughters, Daphne and Doris, who now hold the shares in the Company 50:50 each. It was as if Delia was a buffer between Daphne and Doris, for although the shop was often tense as the two sisters asserted their own ideas as to the future of the shop, relations proceeded on a reasonably even keel. While Daphne has been happy to develop the business as essentially a retail operation attracting passing customers, building regulars and internet customers, Doris has for some time been keen on developing a wholesale business to supply hotels for weddings and undertakers for funerals. She wants ultimately to sell the shop and cash in on the property boom. When Delia retired the sisters’ relationship deteriorated rapidly and led ultimately to a screaming match in front of a shop full of customers; Daphne had sold the last orchid to a customer which Doris had set aside for a wedding order placed by a hotel with which she was trying to develop a relationship. Since then Daphne has not passed on messages to Doris from hotels and has been hanging up the telephone on undertakers, whom she believes too creepy to converse with. For months, the sisters have not been on speaking terms, and last week Doris sent Daphne a solicitor's letter wherein Doris claimed oppression by Daphne. The letter threatens the institution of legal proceedings for oppression and the winding up of the Company unless Daphne agrees to sell her 50% interest in the Company's share capital for a price to be determined as its market value by the Company's auditors. You are required to advise Daphne as to the law applicable to oppression under section 212 of the Companies Act 2014 and winding-up on just and equitable ground under section 569(1)(e) of the Companies Act 2014. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: Big Bank plc (the "Bank") made a loan of €5m to Mighty Motors Limited (the "Company") on the security of a fixed charge over its car showroom and garage in Tallaght County Dublin and a floating charge over its undertaking and assets. The security document provides that upon the passing of a resolution or presentation of a petition to wind up the company, the floating charge will crystallise. After the Bank failed to make any payments to the Revenue Commissioners for three months, the Revenue Commissioners presented a petition to have the Company wound up. You have been approached by the liquidator who tells you that the Bank is claiming to have a fixed charge over 20 high value second-hand cars which were on the Company's premises, on the grounds that the floating charge crystallised when the Revenue Commissioners presented the petition to have the Company wound up. The Revenue Commissioners are however claiming that they are entitled to the cars on the grounds that they have priority over floating charges. You are required to advise the liquidator on the foregoing matter. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

This near miss has caused Pat to consider whether being a sole trader is such a good idea. Additionally, Paul, Pat's other employee, has been pressing Pat to give him an interest in the business. Pat approaches you for advice, seeking information on (a) the advantages and disadvantages of forming a company and (b) the consequences of incorporation for Pat's business. You are required to answer both (a) and (b) and in your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text:
David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their gate mother Madge. They received their shares from Madge under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy let the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were woNh.
David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company’s constitution, you see that 95(1) applies: the directors of a company may in their absolute discretion and without assigning any reason for doing so, decline to register the transfer of any share; the directors' power to decline to register a transfer of shares (other than on account of a matter specified in subsection (2)) shall cease to be exercisable on the expiry of 2 months after the date of delivery to the company of the instrument of transfer of the share." You are required to advise David and Deborah on their lights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text:
