questions.description: Foreign Holidays Ltd (the ‘company’) has operated as a travel agency organising trips, primarily to Iceland for a number…
questions.text: Foreign Holidays Ltd (the ‘company’) has operated as a travel agency organising trips, primarily to Iceland for a number of years. Business was very badly affected by the volcanic activity there which disrupted travel not only in Iceland but also across Europe. Sean and Seamus, the Company’s directors and 50:50 shareholders decided to diversify and last year invested in new offerings – cruises to the Mediterranean and city breaks in Paris. Having established contacts and been on a number of fact-finding cruises and visits to Paris, Seam and Seamus launched their new offerings in October last and had a great many enquiries from people who were interested in making bookings. In addition, in the last number of weeks a significant number of bookings to both destinations were made for mid 2016. The timing of the launch of the new offerings could not have been worse because a cruise ship sank over Christmas and on top of that Paris was the subject of a terrorist attack. These events prompted large scale cancellations – permitted under the Company’s contracts with customers of done 3 months before the cruise. Sean and Seamus have reviewed the Company’s books of account and see that the Company’s books of account and see that the Company’s financial position is hopeless. The company has begun to let go all 10 employees as it could not continue paying their wages and to make ends meet, has even dipped into the PAYE/PRSI it retained from its employees instead of remitting it to the revenue commissioners. The rent is also overdue on the Company’s premises and cruise companies and other suppliers are now agitating for  payment for bookings made by th4e Company which will not now be fulfilled on account of the cancellations by its customers. Ascertaining the true position of the Company’s debtors and creditors has not been made any easier by the fact that a fire destroyed a large number of its records and Sean and Seamus have to rely for the most part on memory and the organisers on their smart phones to reconstruct the company’s records. Sean and Seamus estimated that the company’s debts exceed its assets by some €2 million and took the decision to liquidate. Having placed the company into creditor’s voluntary winding up and having appointed a liquidator, Sean and Seamus are so disgusted that they are thinking of emigrating from Ireland without waiting around any longer. You are required to advise Sean and Seamus on the possibility that they may be the subject of an application for restriction under Section 819 of the Companies Act 2014 and in particular the defences that will be available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: You are approached for advice by Martin, an official working for Big Bank Plc (the ‘bank’) which has been asked to make …
questions.text: You are approached for advice by Martin, an official working for Big Bank Plc (the ‘bank’) which has been asked to make loan facilities available to two companies in the following circumstances: Tony’s Timber Ltd (the Ltd company) was an existing private company which, following the commencement of the Companies Act 2014 converted to a ‘private limited company by shares’ governed by Parts 1 to 14 of the Act and adopted a new single document constitution which adopted the optional provisions in that Act. It wishes to borrow €1,500,000 from the Bank. The purpose of the loan is to enable the ltd Company acquire a farm. Prior to its conversion, the LTD Company had operated a timber mill which was its main object but of course now, it no longer has an objects clause. Jill’s Fashions Public Limited Company (the PLC Company) also wishes to borrow €5,000,000. Its main object in its objects clause is the manufacture of ladies’ fashion clothing. It however, now wishes to borrow the money to buy a factory that manufactures dog food. Martin specifically seeks your advice in relation to the Capacity (i.e. vires) of the Ltd Company and of the PLC Company to borrow the money from the bank for the stated purposes. You are also specifically required to advise on the extent to which, if any, the bank should enquire as to whether the lending is ultra vires the Ltd Company and the PLC Company. In answering this question, you are required to cite all relevant case law and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: ‘Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as t…
questions.text: ‘Section 158(1) of the Companies Act 2014 has replaced Regulation 80 of Part 1 of Table A to the Companies Act 1963 as the cornerstone of corporate governance’. You are required to discuss this statement and in answering this question you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: ‘The Companies Act 2014 makes three very significant changes to the registration of company charges: first, in relation …
questions.text: ‘The Companies Act 2014 makes three very significant changes to the registration of company charges: first, in relation to the definition of charges that are required to be registered under s409 of the Act, secondly by providing for an optional two stage registration process; and thirdly, be legislating for the priority of charges created by companies’. You are required to discuss this statement citing all case and statute law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie’s Freakish Fish Ltd (the Company) which operates a pet shop in Naas …
questions.text: Freddie is sole director and shareholder in Freddie’s Freakish Fish Ltd (the Company) which operates a pet shop in Naas specialising in the sale of exotic fish and related items such as fish tanks, fish food etc. During the last 6 months the Company’s business has been going downhill and Freddie, seeing the writing on the wall, did a number of things: He removed stock to the value of €50,000 from the Company’s premises and purported to sell it to his wife Freda who has her own pet shop in Kildare although it does not appear that Freda paid for the stock; He caused the company to pay off a debt of €75,000 which had been loaned to it by his father, Jeremy. Last month, the Revenue Commissioners (who are owed €250,000) successfully petitioned the High Court to have a liquidator appointed. The liquidator approaches you for advice on the remedies open to him under the Companies Act 2014 in relation to two dispositions identified in both circumstances. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “The general rule that fiduciary duties are owed by company directors to the company (and to the company alone) is so we…
questions.text: “The general rule that fiduciary duties are owed by company directors to the company (and to the company alone) is so well established and acknowledged that it barely admits any exception”. Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “company”), establ…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “company”), established by their late mother Madge, and acquired by them under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and it’s only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not has any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend with the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they’d be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah have come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town where the Company had its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked to register as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company’s articles of association, you see the following provision: “the directors may, in their absolute discretion, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register any transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share”.

questions.description: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in lunch Time Treats Limited (The “Company”) which is in the b…
questions.text: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in lunch Time Treats Limited (The “Company”) which is in the business of supplying fresh pre-packed sandwiches, rolls, salads and other foodstuffs to local shops which do not have the facilities for making-up their own produce. The Company’s business is holding up reasonably well in the recession but their greatest headache is in getting paid for goods supplied. The directors decide that they are going to take a harder line with the shops to which they supply food. It is decided that where payment of a sum in excess of €15,000 is overdue for 6 months, the Company will issue such shops (that are themselves limited companies) with a 21-day letter demanding payment and in the event of non-compliance, the Company will present a petition to have the debtor company would up on the grounds of being unable to pay its debts. You are required to advise Richard and Judy on the law relating to the winding up of companies on the grounds of being unable to pay its debts. You are required to identify possible issues that might arise in the context of the facts provided. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: ‘A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations.’ You are required to discuss this statement…
questions.text: ‘A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations.’ You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: ‘The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and disti…
questions.text: ‘The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and distinct from that of its members remains the general rule of law in Ireland to which there are a number of exceptions”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and stature law in your answer.

questions.description: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the ‘Bank’) to be receiver to Totally PC…
questions.text: Harry is an insolvency practitioner and was recently appointed by Big Bank plc (the ‘Bank’) to be receiver to Totally PC Coffee Limited (the ‘Company’) on foot of a debenture the Company had given to the Bank to secure its borrowings. The Bank is owed over €2,000,000 by the Company and the Company’s principal asset is a unit in an industrial estate in North County Dublin where it mixes, packages and distributes coffee beans sourced from a fair trade plantation in Brazil. The unit was acquired by the Company for €2,500,000 in 2007. Harry believes that the premises is currently worth in the region of €1,500,000 and has a firm offer for that amount. There is another interested party who has said to Harry that he might be prepared to offer €1,900,000 in another three months when he can raise the cash. Harry is also receiving telephone calls on a daily basis from Richard, the managing director and sole shareholder in the Company asking Harry to make sure he gets the best price for the property. Harry is aware that Richard gave a personal guarantee to the Bank  and is aware that if there is a shortfall between the price received for the property and the amount owing the Bank, he as guarantor will be liable in that amount. You are required to advise Harry on his duties as a receiver having particular regard to the duties owed to the Company and also whether he owes any separate duties to a guarantor. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Retail Motors Limited (the “Company”) is in dire financial straits. For some months now, its shareholders and directors,…
questions.text:
Retail Motors Limited (the “Company”) is in dire financial straits. For some months now, its shareholders and directors, John and Timothy, have realised that the Company’s used-car business is hopelessly insolvent. The Company owes €750,000 to the Revenue Commissioners in VAT, PAYE and PRSI; it owes €200,000 on its bank overdraft and also owes in the aggregate €500,000 to other creditors, comprised of suppliers and customers.  The Company’s assets are €275,000, comprised of cars worth approximately €250,000 and about €25,000 in cash. John and Timothy have been dodging their creditors all summer but two of them have been particularly tenacious, one a customer and the other a trade creditor. The Customer (Fred) is owed €15,000 for a car he sold the Company. Fred, who is an old school friend of Timothy, has been telephoning him and calling around to his house on a nightly basis seeking payment. Eventually Fred caught up with Timothy and after a heated conversation when Fred actually pushed Timothy against a wall and warned him of the consequences of not paying him the money, Timothy capitulated and paid Fred the €15,000 owed to him.
On being told by Timothy of what had happened, John decided that they must pay the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation and initiated this the following day when a resolution was passed to place the company into creditors’ liquidation. The trade creditor was a firm that has previously carried out repair work to used cars for the Company. This firm was owed €40,000 and its Managing Director (Mike) had become increasingly abusive to John and Timothy, and most recently to Timothy’s wife June whom he badly frightened after he made a telephone call to her home. In consequence of this, the day after the resolution was passed to put the Company into creditors’ voluntary winding up, Timothy gave a cheque to Mike, drawn from the Company’s already overdrawn bank account. You are required to advise the Company’s liquidator on the validity of the two payments made by the Company: (i) the first for €15,000 to Fred and (ii) the second for €40,000 to Mike’s firm. Your answer must clearly distinguish between (i) and (ii) and you must cite relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: The liquidator of Joe’s Joinery Limited (The “Company”) contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has ju…
questions.text: The liquidator of Joe’s Joinery Limited (The “Company”) contacts you for advice. The liquidator tells you that he has just been appointed to the Company by the high court on the petition of the Revenue Commissioners and needs your help in unravelling the Company’s affairs and determining the order of priorities. Specifically, you are advised that: A company called Raw Materials Limited has a retention of title clause in its contract for the sale of three batches of timber it supplied to the Company. The Liquidator advises you that there are only two batches untouched as the third was made into furniture. The clause in question provided “The property of the timber shall not transfer to the Company until all sums owing to the Vendor have been paid and until then any goods made from the timber shall be the property of the Vendor”. The Liquidator wants to know whether he must return the timber and the furniture made therefrom. Another supplier claims to have a fixed charge over one of the Company’s bank accounts. On further enquiry the Liquidator was shown a copy of a deed of fixed charge over the Company’s book debts which required them to be paid into a designated bank account. As far as the Liquidator can see the account was operated by the Company whose directors lodged and withdrew monies therefrom at will. The liquidator wants to know whether this charge gives the supplier priority over the Revenue Commissioners. The Company created a fixed charge over a site of land in favour of Small Bank plc to secure an overdraft it had with the Bank. It transpires that the charge which was only created 15 days’ ago, does not appears to have been registered in the Companies Registration Office. The liquidator wants to know whether this charge can still be registered and the consequences for Small Bank plc if it is not registered. You are required to advise the Liquidator in respect of each of the foregoing matters. Your answer should clearly distinguish the three cases outlined in (a), (b) and (c) and you must cite all relevant cases and stature law.

questions.description: “The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and disti…
questions.text: “The principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC 22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and distinct from that of its members remains the general rule of law in Ireland to which there are a number of exceptions.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to the Companies Act to have the director of an insolvent company rest…
questions.text: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to the Companies Act to have the director of an insolvent company restricted, the director's only hope of not being restricted is if he can show that the director acted honestly and responsibly". You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company") and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given…
questions.text: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company") and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given his 10% of the shares in the Company by his two fellow shareholding directors as a reward for his many years of service with the Company. The Company is in the printing business and Charlie is the chief typesetter who looks after the printing. Charlie's fellow directors, Richard and Martin each hold 45% of the issued shares in the Company. At the prompting of his brother-in-law, in whom Charlie has been confiding, Charlie comes to you for advice after learning that his fellow directors have recently transferred a printing press out of the Company to a friend of theirs in return for €50,000, which seems to be its market value.  · Charlie tells you that the Company never really had formal board meetings and that things were no different on this occasion, Richard and Martin simply taking it upon themselves to give effect to the transfer. Charlie has explained that any time he has objected to this course of action, Richard and Martin have reminded him that once they agree to a course of action, there is little point in having a board meeting since the board acts by majority and he would be outvoted. They also remind him that as they hold 90% of the shares they can remove him as a director if they see fit so he has little reason to complain. You are required to advise Charlie on the law relating to minority shareholder oppression and the remedies available to a member of a company, and say whether in your opinion Charlie has a case against the Company or its directors. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Paul is the director of City Cars Limited (the “Company”) which has operated a business in North County Dublin. Paul is …
questions.text: Paul is the director of City Cars Limited (the “Company”) which has operated a business in North County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed by Simon although Paul has worked in the Company for 1O years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a company director with responsibility for sales five years ago. Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been "double-jobbing" for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Ludmila. Simon heard from a friend that Paul has been buying cars that were traded in to the Company at a discounted price and selling them on for a significant profit on his website. He also used the Company's workshop to service them before re-sale, again availing of discounts on .the time and materials charged. You are required to advise Simon on the law relating to directors' fiduciary duties and on the case against Paul. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts presented above.

questions.description: “It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either three ways: …
questions.text: “It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly by an estoppel created with their unanimous informal agreement.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant cases and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, an official liquidator who was appointed to the Plot Gets Thicker Limite…
questions.text:
You are approached for legal advice by Gilbert, an official liquidator who was appointed to the Plot Gets Thicker Limited (the "Company"), which had been a sub-contractor in .the film production business. Gilbert was appointed liquidator on foot of a petition to have the Company wound up for failure to pay its debts which had been brought by the Revenue Commission. It appears that the Company is hopelessly insolvent having debts in excess of € 15,000,00 of which some €8,000,00 is owed to the Revenue Commissioners. Following his appointment, Gilbert began to investigate the Company’s affairs and the behaviour of the Company's sole-shareholder and managing director, Reynard Fox ("Mr Fox"). Gilbert tells you: The Company's books and records are in a sorry state. Record keeping has been at best sporadic, and Gilbert is finding it difficult to piece together the Company’s financial history. It appears the film extras were often paid in cash and kept off the books of the Company as there are no records of people having being paid. However, other documents suggest that the Company had supplied hundreds of extras in a recent film in which it had been involved. Basic invoice keeping also appears to have been ignored and there is little or no financial documentation to support the claims which a number of creditors have lodged against the company for both goods and services allegedly supplied. The Company appears to have either gifted or at best made a loan of some €150,000
to Mr Fox which he seems to have used to purchase a Bentley car in his own name. The Company has also incurred massive debts with other film production companies. Its modus operandi seems to have been to agree to secure the acting services of big-name stars for up-coming films in return for the production companies paying the Company a significant fee of between €500,000 and €1,000,000. Gilbert has discovered that the Company was a party of no fewer than 6 such agreements with 6 different production companies and while it received some €5,000,000 from this business, it never once managed to procure the star for the film. The 6 creditors in question have complained to Gilbert that the Company and Mr Fox traded fraudulently or, at best, .recklessly in carrying on this business. Gilbert has found no evidence that the Company even sought the services of the stars in question, after taking the creditors' money. You are required to advise Gilbert on the grounds for applying to the High Court to have Reynard Fox made personally liable for some or all of the Company's debts. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of ORK Limited (the "Company"), the other director of which is h…
questions.text:
Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of ORK Limited (the "Company"), the other director of which is his wife, Deirdre. The Company operated the business of travel agent for a number of years. The Company has loan facilities from Big Bank pie which are secured by a floating charge over the Company's assets, a fixed charge over its leasehold interest in the premise it rents and the personal guarantees of its directors, Damien and Deirdre. The last six months were the worst ever experienced by the Company which made nearly all of its employees redundant. Wages were owed to some of the employees and one of them, Sean, was constantly calling to the family ·home of Damien and Deirdre ·seeking payment. In the last few weeks, the Revenue Commissioners petitioned to have the company wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts and the High Court granted that petition, appointing Jack Harmer the official liquidator. You are retained by Jack to advise as to the law relating of the following payments by the Company: Christmas week, when Sean called at the directors' home seeking payment, Damien was out and Deirdre, who was concerned at Sean's ever-threatening manner, wrote him a cheque for €5,000 in respect of wages owed to him; The morning that the petition was heard by the High Court, Damien transferred €30,000 from one account the Company had into its overdrawn current account ; The  day  after  the  petition  was  presented,  the  Company's  bank  debited  the Company’s current account for €10,000 in respect of accrued interest in the Company’s loans. A month after the petition to wind up the Company, the liquidator himself made a payment from the Company 's overdrawn account to the Company 's landlord in respect of the premises it occupied as otherwise its lease would have been forfeited . You are required to advise Jack the liquidator on the law in relation to each of the four
payments, distinguishing (a), (b), (c) and (d) in your answer. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The fact that certain types of retention of title clauses must be registered in the Companies Registration Office in or…
questions.text:
"The fact that certain types of retention of title clauses must be registered in the Companies Registration Office in order to be valid whereas certain other types of such clauses need not be r registered illustrates that not all security interests created by companies are required to be registered under the Companies Act in order to be valid."
You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.
March 2015

questions.description: “Although the Courts in Ireland have consistently re-iterated the principle in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22…
questions.text: “Although the Courts in Ireland have consistently re-iterated the principle in Salomon v A Salomon & Co Ltd [1897] AC 22, the Irish courts have also recognised that the veil of incorporation can, in appropriate circumstances, be lifted and the Irish legislature has also provided for cases where the separate legal personality of a company will be disregarded”. Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law having particular regard to the reference therein to the Irish courts and Irish legislature.

questions.description:
Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the “Company”) which operates a car sales business
in South County Dublin. Paul …
questions.text:
Paul is a director of Cars4All Limited (the “Company”) which operates a car sales business
in South County Dublin. Paul is not a sharehoider in the Company which is owned by a
family consortium headed-up by Simon although Paul has worked in the Company for 10
years; first as a car salesman before being promoted on a number of-occasions until finally
being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company, approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been “double-jobbing” for the last two years selling second-hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Simon heard from a friend that -Paul has been buying cars that were traded in to the Company at a discounted price and selling them on for a significant profit on his website. He also used the Company’s workshop to service them before re-sale, again availing of discounts on the time and materials charged. You are required to advise Simon on the law relating to directors’ common law and equitable fiduciary duties and on the Company’s remedies against Paul and Jackie. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: “Corporate contractual capacity can be classified under the headings of substantive objects, express powers and implied …
questions.text:
“Corporate contractual capacity can be classified under the headings of substantive objects, express powers and implied powers; any corporate activity outside a company’s objects and powers is ultra vires and void.” You are required io discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your
answer.

questions.description: Jack and Jill are directors and 50:50 shareholders in a company called J&J Acquisitions Limited (the “Purchaser Company”…
questions.text:
Jack and Jill are directors and 50:50 shareholders in a company called J&J Acquisitions Limited (the “Purchaser Company”) which intends to purchase all of the issued share capital in another company called Investment Properties Limited (the “Target Company’). The Target Company owns an apartment building containing 5 apartments which are leased to tenants. The Purchaser Company agrees to buy the shares in the Target Company from Richard (who is the sole owner of all of the shares in the Target Company) for €3 million. Jack and Jill have secured loan facilities with Big Bank pic (the “Bank”) under which the Bank will loan the Purchaser Company €2.5 million. The Purchaser Company has €500,000 of its own funds to make up the difference between the loan and the purchase price of the shares. The-Bank issues a loan agreement addressed to the Purchaser Company which will be the borrower. As security for the loan, the Bank requires: the guarantee of the Target Company, and a charge from the Target Company over the apartment building. The loan agreement also states that the Bank’s solicitor must be satisfied that the loan agreement and the security for the loan (the guarantee and legal mortgage and charge) comply with section 60 of the Companies Act 1963 (the "1963 Act”) and section 31 of Companies Act 1990 (the "1990 Act”). You are required to advise Jack and Jill: on how the guarantee and charge might contravene section 60 of the 1963 Act and section 31 of the 1990 Act, and on how the transaction can be structured so as to be in compliance with those
provisions. : You are required to answer (a) and (b). Both parts carry equal marks. In your answers you
are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “The circumstances in which a retention of title clause will be found to constitute a charge which, if not registered pu…
questions.text:
“The circumstances in which a retention of title clause will be found to constitute a charge which, if not registered pursuant to section 99 of the Companies Act 1963, will be void as against a liquidator or creditor of a company, are now well settled.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your
answer.

questions.description: William's World of Colours Limited (the “Company”) is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributin…
questions.text:
William's World of Colours Limited (the “Company”) is a company engaged in the business of manufacturing and distributing paint to the Irish construction industry. For the past number of years, business has been booming. Two of the Company's customers have had lucrative contracts for the painting of new housing estates. The Company has three directors - William, Peggy and Eugene - and is a single-member private limited company, all shares being owned by William. William is the son of the Company's founder and is the managing director. Peggy, his daughter, is the Sales Director and Eugene is her husband. Eugene is a non-executive
director who works as a local self-employed solicitor. His involvement with the Company is
very limited and he never attends meetings of the directors. In recent times the Company's fortunes have faltered. Both of its major customers have
gone into liquidation, owing the Company several-hundred thousand euro. Both customers
were always slow payers, but the Company received nothing from them for months although it continued to supply them with paint. William realised things were bad when the Company's bank manager telephoned him to say
that the Company had exceeded its overdraft. William persuaded the bank to extend the
overdraft to pay wages and give him time to collect some outstanding debts. The Company
chugged along for another month but then William realised that the Company was insolvent
and without any hope of an immediate improvement. At that point William placed’ the
Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation. The Company's liquidator discovered that the Company's record keeping was sporadic and
that management accounts had not been prepared with the frequency that would have
alerted the Company’s directors to the fact its two main customers has not been discharging
what was owed by them for some time. You are required to advise on the likelihood that each of the three directors will be made the subject of a restriction order pursuant to Section 150 of the Companies Act 1990. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description:
You are approached by Larry, the official liquidator appointed to Moe's Motel Limited (the
“Company”) which was put into…
questions.text:
You are approached by Larry, the official liquidator appointed to Moe's Motel Limited (the
“Company”) which was put into compulsory liquidation after the Revenue Commissioners
successfully petitioned to have it wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its
debts. The Company's main asset was a building in the centre of the town of Moat, County
Westmeath from which it operated a 50 bedroom hotel. Business had been good until a new
motorway was constructed on the Dublin to Galway road, by-passing Moat. Thereafter, the
Company’s business fell-off significantly, resulting in the failure to remit PAYE, PRSI and
VAT to the Revenue Commissioners who are now owed €3.75m. Indeed, all contact
between the Revenue Commissioners and the Company's directors, Moe and May, ceased
and it was this lack of communication which prompted the petition to wind up the Company. Following his appointment, Larry went to the Company's hotel premises, and discovered that it had been stripped of furniture, fixtures and fittings. On further investigation, Larry learned that May ‘had opened up a new hotel having formed a new company called May's Motel Limited ("May’s Company”) which is located on the outskirts of Moat, immediately adjacent to the exit to the motorway and visible from the motorway. Larry understands that the new hotel has been equipped with the furniture, fixtures and fittings of the Company. Moreover, a search of the Companies Registration Office disclosed that the Company created a fixed and floating charge over the hotel and its assets in favour of Moe to secure the sum of €500,000 allegedly due to him for management services rendered to the company over many years. It appears also to Larry that the Company's creditors are quite happy to transact business with May's Company because all sums owed to them by the Company were discharged by it. Larry seeks your advice in relation to the following specific matters: Larry values the furniture, fixtures and fittings of the Company which were moved to the hotel owned by May's Company at €500,000. He wants them back and you are required to advise him as to the relevant law on this point. Larry values the Company's hotel at €1.5m. He cannot, however, sell the hotel
because of the charges in favour of Moe. He wants you to advise him’as tc whether
these charges can be sei aside and if they can, the basis for setting them aside. Larry has learned that the Company's largest creditor, a supplier of food, was paid the €230,000 owed to it in the weeks before the Revenue Commissioners presented their petition to have the Company wound up. Larry asks you whether this money can be recovered by him and if it can, the basis for its recovery. You are required to answer (a) and (b) and (c). In your answers you are required to cite all
relevant case and statute law. -

questions.description: A person may become subject to the duties and obligations of a director of an Irish company in either of three ways on b…
questions.text:
A person may become subject to the duties and obligations of a director of an Irish company in either of three ways on being formally appointed a director, on being found by a Court to be a de facto director; or on being found by a Court to be a shadow director. You are required to: describe how each of the foregoing three circumstances can arise, and the nature of the duties and obligations that can result for the person in each of the
three circumstances. You are required to answer (a) and (b). In your answer you are required to cite all relevant
case and statute law.

questions.description: "A company's objects clause and the doctrine of ultravires provide necessary protection for the shareholders and credito…
questions.text: "A company's objects clause and the doctrine of ultravires provide necessary protection for the shareholders and creditors of companies." Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the "Company") in which they are equal 50:50 shareholders. The C…
questions.text: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the "Company") in which they are equal 50:50 shareholders. The Company’s business has been the purchase, development and resale of period houses. The Company has operated very successfully for the last 10 years; Leonard taking responsibility for the purchase and sale of the properties and Larry looking after the development and building works. Over the last five years Leonard had told Larry that he had put together a portfolio of properties for the Company and that he had been purchasing suitable properties as they came to market so as to ensure a steady supply of properties were available for development by Larry. Larry comes to you for advice. He tells you that he has discovered that Leonard had been acquiring properties not only for the Company but for another company owned by Leonard's son, Frederick. Larry suspects that the better properties (acquired more cheaply with greatest potential for a higher resale value) were siphoned off to Frederick's company. Larry tells you that the Company has three properties on its books now which are worth about 25% less than what the Company paid for them a couple of years ago. Larry believes he has identified two other properties which were destined to be acquired by the Company but instead were diverted to Frederick's company. In contrast to the Company's properties, Larry believes that these other properties are now worth at least as much as Frederick paid for them, if not more. Larry also suspects that Leonard has an interest in Frederick's company. You are required to advise Larry of the Company's remedies against Leonard for breach of his duties as director. In your answer you are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations". You are required to discuss this statement…
questions.text: "A share is an intangible accumulation of rights, interests and obligations". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "Unlike meetings of members, the meetings of directors in a company are not heavily regulated by the Companies Acts". Yo…
questions.text: "Unlike meetings of members, the meetings of directors in a company are not heavily regulated by the Companies Acts". You are required to discuss this statement, stating whether you agree or disagree with it and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your views.

questions.description: Jackie's business has failed. For the last 12 years, Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a shopping centre out…
questions.text: Jackie's business has failed. For the last 12 years, Jackie operated a florist shop from a unit in a shopping centre outside of Dublin. The business was conducted through Smell That Limited (the "Company") of which Jackie was the sole shareholder and one of two directors, the other director being Jackie's former fiance, Ted. Jackie contacted Ted to tell him the company was insolvent and they agreed to hold a directors' meeting with a view to putting the Company into creditors' voluntary winding up. On meeting with Jackie and Ted, you elicit the following information: The Company now owes €120,000 in back rent and taxes. The Company has struggled through the recession, as people have had less disposable income to spend on flowers. The final straw has been an increase in rent in relation to the shop unit the Company rents, as it was increased from €5,000 to €7,000 per month. The Revenue Commissioners have not been paid VAT on sales, or PRSI or PAYE due on Jackie's salary from the Company for three months. In addition, the Revenue Commissioners have issued an assessment for €20,000 in relation to back tax owed to it which the Company cannot afford to pay. The circumstances in which this has become due were that Jackie had hired a teenage girl to work a few hours on a Saturday morning, paying her cash in hand. When this was discovered by the Revenue Commissioners, they claimed for lost PRSI and PAYE tax on the girl's earnings over a two-year period. The Company's books and records have been maintained by a local book-keeper to an acceptable standard, however, on the way to delivering the books and records to the book-keeper for the last 12-month period, Jackie's car was stolen and the books and records destroyed after the joy-rider thieves set fire to the car. Ted, the Company's second director, has not been active in the business of the Company. He agreed to be a director whilst engaged to Jackie because every company needs two directors, but he has not been active in the Company's business, only signing accounts and forms when asked to do so. Although they have since split up, it was amicable and Ted only agreed to stay on until Jackie could find someone else to become a director. You are required to advise both Jackie and Ted on the likelihood that they will be restricted under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 and in particular on the defences available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and m…
questions.text: "Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and more powerful remedy against directors' wrongdoing than any or all of the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle ''. Discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Classical Collection Limited (the "Company'') created a debenture in favour of Big Bank plc to secure a loan of ‚Ç¨5,000…
questions.text: Classical Collection Limited (the "Company'') created a debenture in favour of Big Bank plc to secure a loan of ‚Ç¨5,000,000 made to the Company to purchase and redevelop a building in Galway where it proposed opening a classical music CD shop. The debenture included a first legal mortgage and charge over the building and contained the usual provision allowing Big Bank plc to appoint a receiver. In order to get the loan, Seamus Mahler, its managing director and sole shareholder, was obliged to give a personal guarantee to Big Bank plc. Seamus was happy to do so because his old school friend, Bob, was a local builder who was undertaking the redevelopment and Seamus was very happy that Bob the builder would have fixed his shop to a very high standard. After redeveloping the premises, opening the shop and struggling with dwindling custom, Seamus had to face the reality that there would never be a sufficient number of high spending classical music lovers in Galway city to make such a shop profitable. After defaulting on the Company's loan repayments for two months, Big Bank plc, which was then owed over ‚Ç¨4,500,000, appointed a receiver. Seamus was shocked when he heard through a local auctioneer that the receiver is now proposing to sell the premises, which the Company had acquired for ‚Ç¨5,000,000, for only ‚Ç¨4,500,000 even though substantial works have been carried out to the building. Seamus is now extremely concerned that not only will the Company's property be sold for less than it paid for it, but that Big Bank plc is also likely to call upon his guarantee to the tune of the shortfall, some ‚Ç¨500,000! Bob is also now regretting his re-acquaintance with his old school friend Seamus, as the Company has only paid Bob ‚Ç¨50,000 of the ‚Ç¨500,000 that the redevelopment cost.  Bob believes also that if the receiver could obtain more for the premises, he too could be paid for the building works carried out. You are required to advise the receiver, when disposing of the premises, as to the duties (if any) that he owes to: the Company; the guarantor of the loan, Seamus Mahler; and the Company's largest unsecured creditor, Bob the builder. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: You are approached for advice by Luke who has been appointed the liquidator of Celtic Excesses Limited (the "Company'') …
questions.text: You are approached for advice by Luke who has been appointed the liquidator of Celtic Excesses Limited (the "Company'') which operated as an event management company before the Revenue Commissioners petitioned successfully to have it wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts. Luke seeks your advice in relation to the following specific matters which have come to his attention: the Company's directors, Pat and Ruth, paid-off Pat's brother Timmy who was owed a ‚Ç¨30,000 trade debt by the Company for food his catering company had supplied, about 15 months before the Company was wound up; four months before the Company was wound up, Ruth and Pat caused the Company to transfer its assets to the value of ‚Ç¨300,000 to another company which they own, at nil consideration; the day after the petition to wind up the Company was presented, which was two weeks before the Court made the winding-up order, Pat and Ruth paid their employees all sums that were due them in respect of back and holiday pay and bonuses because they were losing their jobs. You are required to advise Luke on the company law relating to each of the three matters identified at (a), (b) and (c) above. Your answer must clearly distinguish the three matters and cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's case and, to a lesser extent, Regulation 6 of the European…
questions.text: "The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand's case and, to a lesser extent, Regulation 6 of the European Communities (Companies) Regulations 1973 (SI 163 of 1973) provide a satisfactory solution to the practical problems caused by the fact that although companies are separate legal persons, they can only act through human beings." Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods f…
questions.text: Sean has for the last 5 years operated as a sole trader as a wood turner, making wooden bowls and assorted other goods from wood. He employs six people and his business has been very successful. Sean comes to you for advice and during the course of the consultation he tells you a number of things about his business: Sean is considering incorporating the business but is not sure of the advantages and disadvantages of doing this. He is about to acquire a new factory outlet, whereby the products will be made in the same location as they are sold and members of the public can come and see him and his employees at work. Sean will need to borrow from his bank to acquire the new premises. Recently, one of his employees, Bill, received a nasty cut from a chisel which severed an artery and caused him to be off work for two months, and although Sean's insurance company paid out on the claim, Sean had a few sleepless nights worrying over the claim until he received their letter confirming it was covered in full by the policy. Sean's longest serving employee, Fred, has for some time been pressing Sean to form a partnership with him so he can share in the success of the business. Although Sean is agreeable to giving Fred a slice of the action, he is very uncomfortable with forming a partnership with Fred. Whereas Sean has a book-keeper he has never bothered having his accounts audited. You are required to advise Sean on the advantages and disadvantages of incorporating his business as a private company limited by shares, having particular regard to the matters raised by Sean during the consultation. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), establ…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the "Company"), established by their late mother Madge, and acquired by them under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy came home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she had sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy transferring her 50 shares to him, and he asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company's articles of association, you see that Regulation 3 of Part II of Table A applies: "The directors may, in their absolute discretion, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register any transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share". You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "While the traditional position has been that company directors owe their duties to their company and to the company alo…
questions.text: "While the traditional position has been that company directors owe their duties to their company and to the company alone, increasingly it is being recognised that directors can in particular circumstances owe duties to other persons too." Discuss this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "When a liquidator brings an application under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have a de jure director, a de fa…
questions.text: "When a liquidator brings an application under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have a de jure director, a de facto director or a shadow director of an insolvent company restricted, the making of an order will be inevitable unless the person can satisfy the High Court that he or she has acted honestly and responsibly." You are required to identify and discuss the key legal issues raised by this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareh…
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the "Company") which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John's solicitor's practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue for John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John's recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month. For the last 6 months, however, this has fallen steadily and for the last month there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder, especially having regard to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent, and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John's attitude, they would not make any referrals to him - a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise on the following matters: whether John has a remedy under section 205 of the Companies Act 1963; and whether your answer would be different if the articles of association contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them; and whether your answer would be different if a shareholders' agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders' businesses by referring clients to them. Your answer must address each of the three questions at (a), (b) and (c) and must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard entered into a contract with Construct & Develop Limited (the "Company") to level a site that he owns and to con…
questions.text: Richard entered into a contract with Construct & Develop Limited (the "Company") to level a site that he owns and to construct a multi-story car park on the site. Richard was required to pay the Company €4.5 million to carry out the works, to be paid in three equal instalments of €1.5 million each. Richard was concerned at having to pay such a large sum to the Company and it was agreed that he would be given some comfort in the nature of a charge over a massive crane that the Company owns. Accordingly, it was provided in the agreement that "as security for the performance of the Company 's obligations under this contract, at all times when the Company's crane is on the site, it shall be deemed to be the property " of Richard. Within weeks of paying the Company its first instalment of €1.5 million and without any work having been carried out to the site, the Revenue Commissioners successfully petitioned the High Court to have the Company wound up for failure to pay its debts. It transpires that the Company was hopelessly insolvent and owed the Revenue Commissioners €6 million and despite having paid them the bulk of the €1.5 million, this had not prevented the Revenue Commissioners from petitioning to have the Company wound up. The official liquidator, after informing Richard of his appointment, sought to make an arrangement to have the Company's crane removed from Richard's property. Richard refuses to allow the liquidator onto the site and seeks to claim ownership of the crane, which he proposes to sell to defray the €1.5 million he paid to the Company. The official liquidator's response is that any interest in the crane that Richard might have is a charge that is void for want of registration under section 99 of the Companies Act, 1963 (the "1963 Act"). You are required to advise Richard: whether the agreement with the Company created a charge and if so, whether it is a floating charge or a fixed charge and if the agreement did create a charge, whether it was registrable under section 99 of the 1963 Act and the consequences of non-registration of a registrable charge. Your answer must clearly distinguish between (a), (b) and (c) of this question and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Making a winding-up order on the ground that a company is unable to pay its debts is discretionary: not only should a p…
questions.text: "Making a winding-up order on the ground that a company is unable to pay its debts is discretionary: not only should a petitioner satisfy the statutory requirements of section 214 of the Companies Act 1963 but so too should a petitioner be able to show that the debt is bona fide owed to them by the company and that the company does not have a valid cross-claim." You are required to identify and discuss the key aspects of this statement. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: "Section 8 of the Companies Act 1963 and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 provide only limited protection to persons deali…
questions.text: "Section 8 of the Companies Act 1963 and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 provide only limited protection to persons dealing with companies which enter into contracts that are outside of their objects clauses or, ultra vires." You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case law in your answer.

questions.description: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have the director of an in…
questions.text: "When a liquidator brings an application pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have the director of an insolvent company restricted, the director's only hope of not being restricted is if he can show that he acted honestly and responsibly''. You are required to discuss this statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Clare and Maura are directors of, and 50:50 shareholders in, Retail Rodents Limited (the "Company'') which operates the …
questions.text: Clare and Maura are directors of, and 50:50 shareholders in, Retail Rodents Limited (the "Company'') which operates the pet shop business, specialising in rabbits, domestic rats, mice and other small animals. The Company's shop is located in an affluent suburb in Cork and business has generally held up throughout the economic recession. Clare and Maura formed the Company after leaving secondary school. Of the two, Maura is more progressive and has tried on numerous occasions to improve the Company's business by marketing drives, but these have been negatively received by Clare who believes that marketing is wasteful and that most business comes from the fact their shop is close to a primary school, as children stop to look in the window and persuade their parents to buy pets for them. For a long time Maura believed that the internet had potential for the Company. Over a number of months Maura prepared a business plan around setting up a website, to be called Mouse-to-Mouse.ie, whereby people from County Cork can buy small pets online by clicking their picture which would then be delivered to them. Maura has projected that the venture would generate a profit of €75,000 per year and was very eager to proceed. On a number of occasions Maura tried to broach establishing a website, but on each occasion Clare's facial expressions made clear to Maura she was sceptical, and Maura never told her the full details or shared the business plan. Encouraged by her boyfriend, Simon, Maura eventually registered her own website which she called Mouse-to-Mouse.ie, and Simon delivered the purchased pets to customers. Maura and Simon set up their own company to run this business. Maura sourced the animals from the same breeders with whom she had built-up a relationship while acting as a director of Retail Rabbits Limited, and obtained the same volume discounts that Company had negotiated with them. As Maura had suspected, the venture was a success and after a year, was generating a profit of €90,000. Maura had never told Clare about her side-line, but when one of the breeders remarked to Clare that the Company's business must be booming due to the numbers of animals he had been supplying to Maura, Clare became suspicious. On confronting Maura and being told about the website and its success, Clare was outraged that Maura would have, as she saw it, abused her position in the Company by establishing a competing business. You are approached by Clare for advice as to whether Maura has been in breach of her duties as a director of the Company and if she has, as to what remedies are open to Clare and the Company. You are required to advise Clare and the Company. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Sean, who is the liquidator of Timber Tables Limited (the "Company'') comes to you for advice. Sean tells you that the C…
questions.text: Sean, who is the liquidator of Timber Tables Limited (the "Company'') comes to you for advice. Sean tells you that the Company had traded as a small joinery making tables from wood before becoming insolvent. Before Sean can realise the Company's assets, he needs to establish what the Company owns. He has received a number of solicitors' letters from suppliers of goods to the Company demanding their return on the grounds that the contracts under which the goods were supplied provided that title to the goods did not pass to the Company. The cases in respect of which Sean wants your advice are: Supplier A demands the return of all timber supplied to the Company, including that which has been used to make furniture by the Company. The retention of title in relation to the timber provided: "Title to the timber shall not pass to the Company until all sums due by the Company to Supplier A under any consignment of timber delivered to the Company have been paid in full”. Supplier A claims that it is owed €150,000 from the Company for timber and that it is entitled to take that value in timber from the Company's storage and, because that is insufficient, to also take tables made from its timber from the showroom. Supplier B demands the return of all bottles of specialised furniture oil which he supplied to the Company and also the proceeds of sale of any furniture oil sold for which the Company has not yet paid Supplier B. The retention of title clause in that case provided: "Ownership of the furniture oil supplied to the Company shall not pass until Supplier B has received in full the sums due to it from the Company and where the Company has sold furniture oil supplied by Supplier B, a trust in favour of Supplier B shall be impressed upon the proceeds of sale of that furniture oil." Supplier B is owed €40,000 by the Company and is claiming all of the remaining bottles of furniture oil on the premises, worth €20,000, as well as another €20,000 from the Company's bank account. Neither Supplier A nor Supplier B delivered particulars of the foregoing contractual provisions to the Companies Registration Office for registration. You are required to advise Sean as to whether either of the foregoing claims are valid or whether Sean can raise section 99 of the Companies Act 1963 on the grounds that the contracts constitute unregistered charges. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law. Your answer must be in two parts and relate to the claims of Supplier A and Supplier B as described in (a) and (b). Each part carries equal marks.

questions.description: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three way…
questions.text: "It is now well recognised that a company's members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement" You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: "While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to t…
questions.text: "While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to transfer its shares, which restriction will usually take the form of either a right of pre-emption on transfer or a discretion vested in the directors to refuse the registration  of share transfers". You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Jason is a creditor of Dusty Deals Limited (the "Company'') which operated an antique shop in Howth in the North of Dubl…
questions.text: Jason is a creditor of Dusty Deals Limited (the "Company'') which operated an antique shop in Howth in the North of Dublin. Jason supplied over €100,000 worth of antiques to the Company for which he has not been paid. Jason had been pressing the Company's Managing Director and sole shareholder Fintan, for payment for some months but to no avail. Finally, Jason was contacted by Fintan and told that the Company was insolvent and that Fintan and his wife, the only other director, had resolved to put the Company into creditors' voluntary liquidation. Jason heard that Fintan's daughter, Freda, opened her own antique shop in Dalkey in the South of Dublin. On visiting that premises, Jason recognised a number of antiques which he had sold to the Company (and for which he has not been paid). On confronting Freda, Jason was told that she had purchased all of her stock from the Company and if he had a gripe, he should take it up with her parents. Separately, Jason heard that another creditor of the Company who was the landlord of the Company's premises had been paid the rent owed to him by the Company and, coincidentally, was also the owner of the premises occupied by Freda in Dalkey. Jason believes that these transactions need to be investigated fully by the Company's liquidator but when Jason realised that the voluntary liquidator appointed by the sole member Fintan, is Dudley, he became very concerned and comes to you for advice before the creditors' meeting is held. Jason explains that Dudley is a first-cousin of Fintan who operates a small book-keeping business and Jason has serious doubts about his knowledge of company law, creditors' remedies and, indeed, the vigour with which Dudley would investigate the Company's affairs. You are required to advise Jason on: Whether he can object to the appointment of Dudley as liquidator at the creditors' meeting and; If Dudley were to be appointed liquidator, whether Jason could have the liquidation converted to an official liquidation. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company'') and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was give…
questions.text: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the "Company'') and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given his 10% of the shares in the Company by his two fellow shareholding directors as a reward for his many years of service with the Company. The Company is in the printing business and Charlie is the chief typesetter who looks after the printing. Charlie's fellow directors, Richard and Martin each hold 45% of the issued shares in the Company. At the prompting of his brother-in-law, in whom Charlie has been confiding, Charlie comes to you for advice after learning that his fellow directors have recently transferred a printing press out of the Company to a friend of theirs in return for €50,000, which seems to be its market value. Charlie tells you that the Company never really had formal board meetings and that things were no different on this occasion, Richard and Martin simply taking it upon themselves to give effect to the transfer. Charlie has explained that any time he has objected to this course of action, Richard and Martin have reminded him that once they agree to a course of action, there is little point in having a board meeting since the board acts by majority and he would be outvoted; they also remind him that as they hold 90% of the shares they can remove him as a director if they see fit so he has little reason to be complaining. You are required to advise Charlie on the law relating to minority shareholder oppression and the remedies available to a member of a company, and say whether in your opinion Charlie has a case against the Company or its directors. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “The different circumstances in which the Courts have disregarded the separate legal personality of companies are many a…
questions.text: “The different circumstances in which the Courts have disregarded the separate legal personality of companies are many and varied.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case law in your answer.

questions.description: Emma and Sarah are the directors and equal shareholders of The Shoe Shop Limited (the “Company”). The Company operated a…
questions.text: Emma and Sarah are the directors and equal shareholders of The Shoe Shop Limited (the “Company”). The Company operated a small chain of four shoe shops which has been in business selling ladies’ fashion shoes for upwards of eight years. They approach you for advice in relation to the following matters: The Company’s objects clause is rather narrow and provides that the main object is “for the retail sale of ladies’ fashion shoes”. While there are many other powers specified in the objects clause, there is no “independent objects clause”. Emma and Sarah have been considering diversifying and they eventually decided to sell fashion clothing for ladies as well as shoes. They ask whether the sale of such clothing is intra vires the Company. The Company’s objects clause provides that the Company has the capacity “to make gratuitous payments of up to €20,000 to any shareholder of the Company”. Emma and Sarah want to know whether this can be relied upon to pay both of them €10,000 each from the company’s capital. The Company has no distributable profits. You are required to answer both (a) and (b), each of which carries 10 marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Joan and David are the only directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the “Company”) which operates an outd…
questions.text: Joan and David are the only directors and equal shareholders in Fresh Air Limited (the “Company”) which operates an outdoor adventure business based in the west of Ireland. The Company owns approximately 50 acres of mountainous terrain on which it operates its business. An opportunity has arisen to acquire a neighbouring mountain from a company owned 50:50 by David and his brother Bart, called DB Limited, which has agreed to sell the mountain to the Company for €400,000 or lease the mountain to the Company for 20 years at a rent of €15,000 per annum. You are required to advise Joan and David on the proposed sale (or lease) of the mountain to the Company from DB Limited, having regard to each of the following: Section 194 of the Companies Act 1963, Sections 29 and 31 of the Companies Act 1990, and Their common law fiduciary duties as directors of the Company. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Retail Motors Limited (the “Company”) is in dire financial straits. For some months now, its shareholders and directors,…
questions.text: Retail Motors Limited (the “Company”) is in dire financial straits. For some months now, its shareholders and directors, John and Timothy, have realised that the Company’s used-car business is hopelessly insolvent.The Company owes €750,000 to the Revenue Commissioners in VAT, PAYE and PRSI; it owes €200,000 on its bank overdraft and also owes in the aggregate €500,000 to other creditors, compromised of suppliers and customers. The Company’s assets are €275,000 comprised of cars worth approximately €250,000 and about €25,000 in cash. John and Timothy have been dodging their creditors all winter, but two of them have been particularly tenacious, one a customer and the other a trade creditor. The customer (Fred) is owed €15,000 for a car which he sold to the Company. Fred, who is an old school friend of Timothy, had been telephoning him and calling around to his house on a nightly basis seeking payment. Eventually Fred caught up with Timothy and after a heated conversation, when Fred actually pushed Timothy against the wall and warned him of the consequences of not paying him his money, Timothy capitulated and paid Fred the €15,000 owed to him. On being told by Timothy of what had happened, John decided that they must put the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation, and they initiated this the following day when a resolution was passed to place the company into liquidation. The trade creditor was a firm that had previously carried out repair work to used cars for the Company. This firm was owed €40,000 and its Managing Director (Mike) had become increasingly abusive to John and Timothy and most recently to Timothy’s wife June whom he badly frightened after he made a telephone call to their home. In consequence of this, the day after the resolution was passed to put the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation, Timothy gave a cheque to Mike, drawn on the company’s already overdrawn bank account. You are required to advise the Company’s liquidator on the validity of the two payments made by the Company: The cash payment of €15,000 to Fred, and The cheque for €40,000 to Mike’s firm. You are required to answer both (a) and (b), each of which carries 10 marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “Unless it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court that the directors of an insolvent company have acted both honestl…
questions.text: “Unless it is proved to the satisfaction of the Court that the directors of an insolvent company have acted both honestly and responsibly in relation to the affairs of that company, they will be restricted pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990.” You are required to discuss this statement. In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.
