questions.text: In what circumstances will a floating charge crystallise? In your answer, you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs an estate agency. They are also shareh…
questions.text: Bob, Patrick, and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs an estate agency. They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John. John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John’s solicitor’s practice for their conveyancing, and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John. John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John’s recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month there were only 5 referrals to him. John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder and pointing to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John’s attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim that they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise John as to the following matters: Whether John has a remedy under section 205 of the Companies Act 1963; and Whether your answer would be different if the articles of association or a shareholders’ agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders’ businesses by referring clients to them. You are required to answer both (a) and (b), each of which carries 10 marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to t…
questions.text:
“While shares in companies are prima facie transferable, a private company is required by law to restrict the right to transfer its shares, which restriction will usually take the form of either a right of pre-emption on transfer or a discretion vested in the directors to refuse the registration of share transfers”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case law in your answer.
March 2013

questions.description: The effect of the amendments to section 33 of the Companies Act 1963 (made by section 7 of the Investment Funds Companie…
questions.text: The effect of the amendments to section 33 of the Companies Act 1963 (made by section 7 of the Investment Funds Companies and Miscellaneous Provisions Act 2005) has been to blur the distinction between private companies and public companies”. Do you agree with this statement? In answering this question you are required to cite all relevant and statue law in support of your answer.

questions.description: “Generally speaking this principle [i.e. that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members] and the statutory r…
questions.text: “Generally speaking this principle [i.e. that a company is a legal entity distinct from its members] and the statutory rules of the company law in which the principle is implicit apply to the relationship between holding companies and subsidiaries and to transactions between them and third parties”. Re Frederick Inns Ltd [1991] ILRM 582 (HC) at 587 (Lardner J) You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statue law in support of your answer.

questions.description: You are required to advise the directors of Crumbs Bakery Limited (the “Company”).  The Company is a private company lim…
questions.text: You are required to advise the directors of Crumbs Bakery Limited (the “Company”).  The Company is a private company limited by shares which has 15 shareholders. Five of the shareholders hold 10 shares each and are the five brothers who incorporated the company to take over the bakery from their father who had operated as a sole trader. The other ten shareholders are employees who each hold 5 shares. The Company has six directors – five of whom are brothers and the sixth is nominated by the employee shareholders. The directors require your advice in relation to the following two applications to have shares registered. The two applications are: One of the employees, Jack died recently and his widow has lodged a stock transfer form electing to be registered as a member of the Company in relation to Jack’s 5 shares. The directors want to know their rights and obligations in relation to deciding whether or not to register Jack’s widow as a member. The second application concerns a request to transfer the 5 shares held by one employee who is retiring (Fred) to a new employee who is joining (Jason). The directors are disposed to facilitating this transfer but have noticed that the stock transfer form has not been stamped by the Revenue Commissioners. You should note that the Company’s articles of association incorporate the Regulations contained in part II of table A. You are required to advise the directors on (a) and (b). Note you must answer (a) and (b), which carry equal marks, separately.  You must cite all relevant case and statute law in your answers.

questions.description: Shelia’s Shelves Limited (the “company”) is in the business of manufacturing and distributing wooden shelves for use in …
questions.text: Shelia’s Shelves Limited (the “company”) is in the business of manufacturing and distributing wooden shelves for use in homes. The Company is a single-member private limited company that is owned by Sheila and its directors are Sheila, her husband Woody, and Jack who is the Company’s marketing manager. Jack has worked for the Company for just under four years and was employed to assist the Company increase its sales by establishing an internet presence. Jack’s endeavours have been very successful and the Company’s turnover in 2009 was three times what it was when Jack was appointed marketing manager. Sheila was so impressed with Jack’s performance and success that she agreed to his appointment to the Board of Directors in 2010. On Jack’s appointment as a director, in addition to his having been given a substantial salary increase, Sheila indicated that she would transfer Jack 10% of the Company’s shares in 2012 if he continued to increase sales. In June 2012 Jack went to Sheila and asked her when he could expect to receive his shares, given that turnover was up 25%. When Sheila was not as forthcoming as Jack would have liked, Jack decided he would consider his options. For the next few weeks Jack carried out private research into setting up his own business. He has sourced a small factory in Poland where the component parts of shelves can be made for 10% of the cost of raw material and labour in Ireland and even allowing for the transportation costs, Jack is convinced that he can have his shelves on sale in Ireland for 60% of the cost of the Company’s shelves. Jack has also started to take advance orders for the shelves from large retail stores that currently give orders to the Company. Last Friday Jack handed his notice to Sheila, resigning from the Company as director and employee. On telling Sheila why he was leaving and what he was going to do, Jack was shocked to be told by Sheila that he would be hearing from the Company’s solicitors in connection with his flagrant breaches of duty as a director of the Company. You are required to advise Jack as to the possible action that the Company might initiate against him and on his options. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “Regulation 80 of part 1 of table A is the cornerstone of corporate governance in Irish companies”. Do you agree with th…
questions.text: “Regulation 80 of part 1 of table A is the cornerstone of corporate governance in Irish companies”. Do you agree with this statement? In answering this question you are required to cite all relevant case and statue law in support of your answer.

questions.description: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line book shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Comp…
questions.text: Irish Books Limited (the “Company”) is an on-line book shop operating from an industrial estate in West Dublin. The Company has a term loan and overdraft from Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a first charge over its premises. Its borrowings are also secured by a separate fixed charge over its book debts. Book debts are paid into a separate account which the Bank can freeze at its discretion. Due to poor turnover, the Company stopped repaying its loans to the Bank which than appointed a receiver. Last month the directors placed the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation. Following his appointment the Company’s liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: Through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the fixed charge over its premises which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under Section 99 of the Companies Act 1963; and The charge over the account into which rent was paid to the Company was registered under section 99 of the Companies Act 1963 in the Companies Registration Office as a fixed charge over the  Company’s book debts; however, the Revenue Commissioners are claiming priority of the due to them ahead of sums due to the Bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer as both parts carry equal marks. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “Section 218 of the Companies Act 1963 is a statutory example of the Pari Passu Principle which requires all creditors o…
questions.text: “Section 218 of the Companies Act 1963 is a statutory example of the Pari Passu Principle which requires all creditors of an insolvent  company to be placed on an equal footing and not “leap frog” the statutory queue ordained by the Companies Acts”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statue law in your answer.

questions.description: Noddy and Gilbert are equal shareholders and directors in Blue Rinse Limited (the “Company”) which has been in the busin…
questions.text:
Noddy and Gilbert are equal shareholders and directors in Blue Rinse Limited (the “Company”) which has been in the business of selling cultural holidays to retired middle class people since 2000.  Business was very strong up until 2009 and whilst the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last few years, business has never been worse following the financial crisis and the plunge in bank shares and consequently the non-payment of dividends to the folk who have traditionally made up its customer base. Existing customers who previously had a number of extended weekends in Paris, Vienna and other cities are staying at home and making good with the annual Wexford Opera festival. Noddy and Gilbert have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on the rent for its premises and its bank loan of some €2,500,000 secured by a mortgage debenture incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge on the Company’s premises which is now worth only €1,750,000. This month is it unlikely to be able to pay to the Revenue Commissioners the full amount of VAT it collected. Noddy and Gilbert believe that if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving as they believe the worst of the recession is over. Noddy has approached an old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company offering him 25% of the Company (12 ½ % each from him and Gilbert). Matters came to a head yesterday when the Company’s bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company’s bank loan being in arrears. Noddy and Gilbert approach you for advice on the following matters: The steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company : The likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company;
And The consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed. You are required to advise Noddy and Gilbert in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b) and (c) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: “Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and m…
questions.text: “Section 205 of the Companies Act 1963 provides the shareholders of a company with a more flexible, more available and more powerful remedy against directors’ wrongdoing than any or all of the exceptions to the Rule in Foss v Harbottle”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: “The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand’s Case and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 are effective in e…
questions.text: “The doctrine of ostensible authority, the Rule in Turquand’s Case and Regulation 6 of SI 163 of 1973 are effective in ensuring the enforceability of a contract that has been entered into by a company, acting by its directors”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Noddy and Gilbert are the only directors and equal 50:50 shareholders in Small Animal Hay Limited (the “Company”) which …
questions.text: Noddy and Gilbert are the only directors and equal 50:50 shareholders in Small Animal Hay Limited (the “Company”) which grows hay and straw and packages it for sale to pet shops as feedstuffs and bedding for rabbits, hamsters and other small animals. The Company owns approximately 2 acres of land in north County Dublin from which it harvests hay and straw. An opportunity has arisen for the Company to acquire a further 2 acres of adjoining land from another company called Home Farm Limited of which Gilbert is the sole shareholder and director with his friend Gerard. The proposed purchase price is €200,000; alternatively, Home Farm Limited is prepared to lease the land to the Company at a rent of €20,000 per annum. You are required to advise all parties on their obligations under: Section 194 of the Companies Act 1963, Sections 29 and 31 of the Companies Act 1990 and Directors’ duties at common law. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Foreign Holidays Limited (the “Company”) has operated as a travel agency organising trips, primarily to Iceland, for a n…
questions.text: Foreign Holidays Limited (the “Company”) has operated as a travel agency organising trips, primarily to Iceland, for a number of years. Business was very badly affected by the volcanic activity there which disrupted travel not only in Iceland but also across Europe. Sean and Seamus, the Company’s directors and 50:50 shareholders decided to diversify and last year invested in a new offering-cruises to the Mediterranean. Having established contracts and been on a number of fact-finding cruises, Sean and Seamus launched their new offering in October last and had a great many enquiries from people who were interested in making bookings. In addition, in the last number of weeks a significant number of bookings were made. The recent sinking of a cruise ship has, however, prompted large scale cancellations permitted under the Company’s contracts with customers if done 6 months before the cruise. Sean and Seamus have reviewed the Company’s books of account and see that the Company’s financial position is hopeless. The Company has begun to let go all 10 employees as it could not continue to pay their wages and to make ends meet, has even dipped into the PAYE/PRSI it retained from its employees instead of remitting it to the revenue commissioners. The rent is also overdue on the Company’s premises and cruise companies are now agitating for payment for bookings made by the Company which will not now be fulfilled on account of the cancellations by its customers. Sean and Seamus estimate that the company’s debts exceed its assets by some €1 million. You are required to advise Sean and Seamus on the possibility that they may be the subject of an application for restriction under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 and in particular the defences that will be available to them. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard recently incorporated his carpentry business which employs eight carpenters; for many years, Richard had operate…
questions.text: Richard recently incorporated his carpentry business which employs eight carpenters; for many years, Richard had operated as a sole trader. The new company is called Wooden World Limited (the “Company”). One of the reasons Richard incorporated his business was because Joe, who has worked for him for 10 years, has been pressing to become his partner. Richard was not anxious to create a partnership and instead has offered him 20% of the shares in the Company for €30,000, which is acceptable to Joe. Richard has agreed with Joe that Joe’s 20% of the shares will confer the right on the holder to be a director and that this would be provided for in the articles of association. Richard comes to you for advice on how to structure the sales of shares. While Richard is very happy to have Joe owning shares in the company, he is concerned that Joe might sell them to someone else and he asks you to outline the options open to him to safeguard his position. Specifically, Richard asks your advice on what might happen if Joe wished to sell his shares to someone Richard did not approve of or what would happen to the shares if Joe died. Joe also asks whether the Company’s articles of association should contain something more than Model Regulation 3 of part II of Table A. You are required to provide Richard with the advice he has sought and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “A floating charge is a charge over a company’s present or future property, or classes of property, which hovers over th…
questions.text: “A floating charge is a charge over a company’s present or future property, or classes of property, which hovers over that property until the moment of crystallisation whereupon the charge fastens onto the charged property or class or property, and becomes a quasi-fixed charge”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Roderick and Robert operate a roofing business and employ eight people. Their work involves putting tiles and slates on …
questions.text: Roderick and Robert operate a roofing business and employ eight people. Their work involves putting tiles and slates on the roofs of houses and industrial premises. By its nature the work is dangerous and despite being committed to safe work practices, there have been several accidents over the years. Most recently one of their employees fell from a roof in Ranelagh and broke his back, leaving him paraplegic. Roderick’s and Robert’s insurance covered the €1,600,000 compensation which was required to be paid to the injured employee however, in addition to a big increase in their insurance premium, Roderick and Robert were told by the insurance company that their cover was been capped at €500,000 for this year. The business is currently organised as a partnership and Roderick and Robert fear that their personal assets may be exposed were there to be another serious accident. You are required to advise Roderick and Robert on the advantages and consequences of incorporating their business as a private company limited by shares. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: George is the liquidator of Second Hand Arts Limited (the “Company”) which has been placed into official liquidation fol…
questions.text: George is the liquidator of Second Hand Arts Limited (the “Company”) which has been placed into official liquidation following the bringing of a petition by the revenue commissioners grounded on the Company’s inability to pay its debts. George seeks your advice on the validity of the following transactions: Eighteen months before the presentation of the petition, the Company had created a floating charge over its stock in trade in favour of its sole member and managing director, John, to secure a loan of €50,000 made by John to the Company. There is evidence that the loan made by John was advanced in cash the day after the floating charge was created. Two cheques were drawn and dated one month before the petition was presented to the High Court to have the Company wound up. The first cheque for €15,000 was payable to John and represented the repayment of part of the loans made 18 months earlier. This cheque was cashed the same day it was drawn. The second cheque for €30,000 was payable to a creditor of the company for goods purchased by the Company and that cheque was cashed the day after the petition was presented. You are required to advise George on the transactions described in (a) and (b) and in your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “The doctrine of ultra vires affords little or no protection to either shareholders or creditors and should be abolished…
questions.text: “The doctrine of ultra vires affords little or no protection to either shareholders or creditors and should be abolished.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: “The Principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and distin…
questions.text: “The Principle in Salomon v Salomon and Co [1897] AC22 that a company has its own legal personality, separate and distinct from that of its members remains the general rule of law in Ireland to which there are a number of exceptions”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Jack and Jill are directors and 50:50 shareholders in a Company called J & J Acquisitions Limited (the “Purchaser Compan…
questions.text: Jack and Jill are directors and 50:50 shareholders in a Company called J & J Acquisitions Limited (the “Purchaser Company) which intends to purchase all the issued share capital in another company called Investment properties Limited (“the Target Company). The target company owns an apartment building containing 10 apartments which it leases to tenants. The purchaser company agrees to buy the shares in the Target Company from Richard (who is the sole owner of all the shares in the Target Company) for €3 million. Jack and Jill have secured loan facilities with Big Bank plc (the Bank) under which the bank will loan the Purchaser Company €2.5 million. The Purchaser Company has €500.000 of its own funds to make up the difference between the loan and the purchase price of the shares. The Bank issues a loan agreement addressed to the Purchaser Company which will be the borrower. As security for the loan, the Bank requires the guarantee of the Target Company and a first legal mortgage and charge from the Target Company over the title to the apartment building. The loan agreement also states that the Bank’s solicitors must be satisfied that the loan agreement and the security of the loan (the guarantee and legal mortgage and charge) comply with section 60 of the Companies Act 1963 (the 1963 Act) and section 31 of Companies Act 1990 (the 1990 Act). You are required to advise the directors of the Purchaser Company, Jack and Jill, on the issues presented by section 60 of the 1963 Act and section 31 of the 1990 Act on how the transaction can be structured so as to be in compliance with those provisions. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “An inspector can be appointed to a company in one of two ways: (a) by the court or (b) by the Director of Corporate Enf…
questions.text: “An inspector can be appointed to a company in one of two ways: (a) by the court or (b) by the Director of Corporate Enforcement. The manner of appointment will determine the scope of the inspection and the powers of the inspector.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “Company) The Comp…
questions.text: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “Company) The Company was established by their late mother Madge and each of them acquire their shares under Madge’s will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company. Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy went home to Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company. They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacts them again and announced that she sold her shares to the Company Joe, a local business man in the same town as where the Company has its premises. A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer form signed by Cindy and asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice. On examination of the Company’s articles of association, you see that Regulation 3 of part II of table A applies: “The directors may, in their absolute, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register ant transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share” You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe. In your answer you are you required to cite all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: “The fact that certain types of retention of title clauses must be registered in the Companies Registration Offices in o…
questions.text: “The fact that certain types of retention of title clauses must be registered in the Companies Registration Offices in order to be valid whereas certain other types of such clauses need not be registered illustrates that not all security interests created by companies are required to be registered under section 99 of the Companies Act 1963.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Paul is a director of Cars 4 All Limited (the “Company”) which operated a car sales business in South County Dublin. Pau…
questions.text: Paul is a director of Cars 4 All Limited (the “Company”) which operated a car sales business in South County Dublin. Paul is not a shareholder in the Company which is owned by a family consortium headed up by Simon although Paul has worked in the Company for 10 years: first as a car sales man before being promoted on a number of occasions until finally being appointed a director of the Company with responsibility for sales. Simon, the majority shareholder and Managing Director of the Company, approaches you for advice. It has come to his knowledge that Paul has been “double-jobbing” for the last two years selling second hand cars through a website fronted by his wife, Jackie. Simon heard from a friend that Paul had been buying cars at discounted price that were traded in to the Company and selling them on for a significant profit on his website. He also used the Company’s workshop to service them before re-sale, again availing of discounts on the time and materials charged. You are required to advise Simon on the law relating to the directors common law and equitable fiduciary duties and on the Company’s remedies against Paul and Jackie. In your answer you are you required to cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in Lunch Time Treats Limited (the “Company”) which is in the b…
questions.text: Richard and Judy are the directors and 50:50 shareholders in Lunch Time Treats Limited (the “Company”) which is in the business of supplying fresh pre-packed sandwiches, rolls, salads and other foodstuffs to local shops which do not have the facilities for making up their own produce. The Company’s business is holding up reasonably well in the recession but their own greatest headache is getting paid for goods supplied. The directors decide that they are going to take a harder line with the shops to which they supply food. It is decided that were payment of a sum in excess of €15,000 is overdue for six months, the Company will issue such shops (that are themselves limited companies) with a 21 day letter demanding payment and in the event of non-compliance, the Company will present a petition to have the debtor company wound up on the grounds of being unable to pay its debts. You are required to advise Richard and Judy on the law relating to the winding up of companies on grounds of inability to pay their debts. You are required to identify possible issues that might arise in the context of the facts provided. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: “Where private companies adopt Regulation 3 of Part II of Table A, the directors will have an absolute discretion in relation to the decision as to whether or not to register a transfer of shares.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.text: “Section 37 of the Companies Act 1963 has greatly simplified the law relating to pre-incorporation contracts.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Ted and Jack are the only two directors and 50:50 shareholders in How’s the Head Now Limited (the “Company”) which has b…
questions.text: Ted and Jack are the only two directors and 50:50 shareholders in How’s the Head Now Limited (the “Company”) which has been in the business of running a shop selling herbal remedies and other products associated with so-called “head-shops”; the company also opened a mail-order business. From the incorporation of the Company in 2006 until last year, business had been very strong. Each year revenue had risen as customers clambered to purchase the products sold in the shop.  However, in May of 2010 the Government introduced new legislation the effect of which was to make the selling of many of the products sold in the Company’s shop illegal.  Sales plummeted over the coming months and Ted and Jack eventually closed the shop in September by causing it to be placed into creditors’ voluntary liquidation. The staff in the shop had been on half wages from May to September, and Ted and Jack stopped remitting VAT and PAYE to the revenue commissioners in June in an attempt to fund the continued operations of the Company.  The Company is also in arrears on the rent for the shop and in total its liabilities exceed its assets by some €500,000.  Ted and Jack would contend that they did everything possible to keep the shop open and had even spent €50,000 on a trip to the Netherlands to see whether they could source any products there which would be considered to be legal in Ireland.  Another €50,000 was spent on legal advice looking for loopholes in the new legislation, and whilst this did identify that certain items that might be of interest to their clients could still be sold, the range of products was greatly reduced and could not save the Company from liquidation.  In addition to these matters, the liquidator discovered that there were literally no records of mail order customers and it appears that either all of their particulars had either never been retained or else had been deleted. The liquidator who has been appointed to the Company approaches you for advice in relation to the application he is required to bring under section 150 of the Companies Act 1990 to have Ted and Jack restricted as company directors.  Specifically, the liquidator wants you to consider whether Ted and Jack might have any defence on the basis that they acted honestly and responsibly. You are required to advise on the liquidator’s specific query and on the law relating to the restriction of directors and in your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law in support of your advice.

questions.description: Benny and Elaine are director and 50:50 shareholders of Imperial Pets Limited (the “Company”) which operates a pet shop.…
questions.text: Benny and Elaine are director and 50:50 shareholders of Imperial Pets Limited (the “Company”) which operates a pet shop.  The shop has been in operation for three years and in that time Benny has confined his involvement to keeping the Company’s books and accounts, dealing with the shop lease, and other matters relating to the administration of the Company.  Elaine, on the other hand, has been involved in operating the shop and selling small animals such as hamsters, guinea-pigs, mice and rabbits.  Elaine has also been responsible for sourcing and buying the animals and has established connections with local breeders.  There was a clear understanding that Benny and Elaine would stick to these responsibilities. The Company’s articles of association permit the appointment of a managing director, but Benny and Elaine never formally passed a board resolution appointing either of them as managing director of the Company. In January, Elaine caught a severe flu which developed into pneumonia and incapacitated her for 4 weeks.  In that time, Benny had to run the shop, and although he never before acquired animals, started buying them.  From a trade magazine called “Rabbit Universal News” he read of a special offer that a breeder of Netherland Dwarf Rabbits had – 3,000 at only €10 each; this was a particularly good price as Benny knew Elaine could sell them for €35 each.  Benny contacted the seller, and telling the seller that he was the Managing Director of the Company, entered into a contract to buy the rabbits for €30,000. On Elaine’s return to work she was horrified at the contract Benny had concluded.  Based on sales over the previous three years of 10 rabbits a week, it would take nearly 6 years to sell 3,000 rabbits! When the rabbit dealer refused to cancel the contract, Elaine contacts you for advice as to whether the contract is enforceable against the Company and, specifically, whether Benny had the authority to bind the company to the contract to buy the 3,000 rabbits. You are required to advise Elaine on her query and in your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Two years ago, Classical Collection Limited (the “Company”) created a debenture in favour of Big Bank plc to secure a lo…
questions.text: Two years ago, Classical Collection Limited (the “Company”) created a debenture in favour of Big Bank plc to secure a loan of €4,000,000 loaned to it to acquire a premises in Galway where it proposed opening a classical music CD shop. The debenture included a first legal mortgage and charge over the premises in Galway and contained the usual provision allowing Big Bank plc to appoint a receiver.  In order to get the loan, Seamus Mahler, its managing director, was obliged also to give a personal guarantee to Big Bank plc. After opening the shop and struggling for two years with dwindling custom, Seamus had to face the reality that there would never be a sufficient number of high-spending classical music lovers in Galway city to make such a shop profitable.  After defaulting on the Company’s loan repayments for two months, Big Bank plc which was then owed over €3,500,000 appointed a receiver.  Seamus was shocked when he heard through a local auctioneer that the receiver is now proposing to sell the premise for only €2,500,000 although it had been acquired by the Company for €3,500,000.  Seamus is now extremely concerned that not only will the Company’s property be sold for less than it paid for it, but Big Bank plc is also likely to call upon his guarantee to the tune of the shortfall, some €1,000,000! Moreover, the Company’s suppliers of CDs are concerned because then, as unsecured creditors, are owed €50,000 and if the premises realised more than that owed to Big Bank plc, they might receive some of what is owed to them. You are required to advise the receiver, in disposing of the premises, as to the duties (if any) that he owes to: the Company; its managing director Seamus Mahler who personally guaranteed the loan from Big Bank plc; and the suppliers of CDs to the Company. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Corporate Caterers Limited (the “Company”) was established in 2006 to provide catering at events sponsored by companies …
questions.text: Corporate Caterers Limited (the “Company”) was established in 2006 to provide catering at events sponsored by companies in the financial services industry. The last number of years have been a struggle as fewer and fewer companies sponsored events where catering was required.  For some months, its shareholders and directors John and Timothy, have realised that the Company is hopelessly insolvent. The Company owes €750,000 to the revenue commissioners in VAT, PAYE and PRSI; it owes €200,000 on its bank overdraft and also owes in the aggregate €500,000 to other creditors, comprised of suppliers of food and beverages and customers.  The Company’s assets are €80,000, comprised of catering equipment worth approximately €75,000 and about €5,000 in cash. John and Timothy have been dodging their creditors all summer but two of them have been particularly tenacious, one a mortgage broker firm owed a €15,000 refund and the other a supplier of beef to the company owed €50,000.  The mortgage broker who is owed €15,000 has been telephoning John, whom he has known for years seeking the return of the money, as his firm is itself in financial difficulties. The mortgage broker has been telephoning John and calling around to his house on a nightly basis seeking payment.  Eventually, and after a heated conversation during which the mortgage broker pushed John against a wall and warned him of the consequences of not paying him his money, John capitulated and paid over the €15,000 owed. On being told of what happened, Timothy decided that they must put the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation and initiated this the following day when a resolution was passed to place the company into creditors’ liquidation. The trade creditor which had supplied beef to the Company was owed €50,000 and its Managing Director had become increasingly abusive to John and Timothy, and most recently to Timothy’s wife June whom he badly frightened after he made a telephone call to their home.  In consequence of this, the day after the resolution was passed to put the Company into creditors’ voluntary winding up, Timothy gave a cheque to the beef supplier, drawn on the Company’s already overdrawn bank account. You are required to advise the Company’s liquidator on the validity of the two payments made by the Company: the payment of €15,000 to the mortgage broker and the payment of €50,000 to the beef supplier. Parts (i) and (ii) carry 10 marks each.  Your answer must clearly distinguish between (i) and (ii) and you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Denis was recently appointed liquidator of Southern Shopping Limited (the “Company”) which owns a chain of 4 supermarket…
questions.text: Denis was recently appointed liquidator of Southern Shopping Limited (the “Company”) which owns a chain of 4 supermarket-type shops in Munster. Denis was appointed liquidator to the Company after the directors placed it into creditors’ voluntary liquidation on account of its insolvency.  In the realisation of the Company’s assets, Denis has received a number of solicitor’s letters from the suppliers of goods to the Company. The letters demand return of goods on the grounds that the contracts under which the goods were supplied provided that title to the goods did not pass to the Company. Denis approaches you for advice in relation to the following cases: Supplier A demands the return of all frozen beef, including that which had been cut-up into joints and steaks and minced into burgers by the Company.  The retention of title in relation to the beef provided: “Title to the beef shall not pass to the Company until all sums due by the Company to Supplier A under any consignment of beef delivered to the Company have been paid in full”.  Supplier A claims that it is owed €380,000 from the Company for beef and that it is entitled to take that amount of beef from the Company’s freezers. Supplier B demands the return of all batteries which it supplied to the Company and also the proceeds of sale of any batteries sold for which the Company has not yet paid Supplier B.  The retention of title clause in that case provided: “Ownership of the batteries supplied to the Company shall not pass until Supplier B has received in full the sums due to it from the Company and where the Company has sold batteries supplied by Supplier B, a trust in favour of Supplier B shall be impressed upon the proceeds of sale of those batteries”.  Supplier B is owed €120,000 by the Company and is claiming all of the batteries on the premises (worth €100,000) as well as another €20,000 from the Company’s bank account. Supplier C demands the return of garden furniture which it supplied to the Company.  Its retention of title clause provided: “Beneficial ownership in the garden furniture supplied to the Company shall not pass to the Company until Supplier C has been paid the sums due to it by the Company”.  It is owed €230,000 by the Company and believes there is furniture worth this amount on the premises. None of the suppliers delivered particulars of the foregoing contractual provisions to the Companies Registration Office for registration. You are required to advise Denis as to whether any of the foregoing claims are valid or whether Denis can raise section 99 of the Companies Act 1963 on the grounds that they constitute unregistered charges. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Hugh and Dermot are directors of and 50:50 shareholders in King Kanute Kars Ltd (the “Company”) which is in the business…
questions.text: Hugh and Dermot are directors of and 50:50 shareholders in King Kanute Kars Ltd (the “Company”) which is in the business of buying and selling second hand cars.  Business has been slow for the last 12 months and Hugh and Dermot have worked hard to sell cars cutting their margins significantly. Hugh’s performance has been considerably better than Dermot and Hugh feels he has been carrying the business since the recession. Hugh’s discontent peaked in July when all 20 sales of cares in the month were made by him. In August, Hugh’s wife Annette formed a new company – Annette’s Wheels Limited – to front a new car sales business for Hugh. Annette purchased stock from outlets that normally sold cars to the Company and always introduced herself as “Hugh’s wife” making it clear that she was connected to Hugh and all purchases were made with the discounts negotiated by Hugh for the Company. Selling cars was the same – Annette would contact people who had previously purchased cars from Hugh through the Company. In August and September the Company’s business was badly affected and its sales were about 20% of what they had been. You are approached by Dermot for advice as to whether Hugh has been in breach of his duties as director of the Company and if he has, as to what remedies are open to Dermot and the Company. You are required to advise Dermot and the Company. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs an estate agency.  They are also shareh…
questions.text: Bob, Patrick and Patty are directors of B-P-P Limited (the “Company”) which runs an estate agency.  They are also shareholders in the Company and each hold 25 of the 100 issued shares, the other 25 shares being held by John.  John is a solicitor and has never been a director of the company, being content to be just a shareholder. The premises from which the Company operates is owned by John and leased to the Company at a very favourable rent for the Company. Bob, Patrick and Patty have traditionally recommended that purchasers and vendors of properties that go through the agency use John’s solicitor’s practice for their conveyancing and this has been a very lucrative source of revenue to John.  John would in turn recommend to his clients that they use the Company as their estate agent. In recent times, however, John’s recommendations have been few and far between and Bob, Patrick and Patty have been recommending other firms of solicitors to clients of the Company as it seems to them that these other solicitors are in a better position than John to make cross-referrals back to the Company. On average, the Company would have made about 30 referrals to John every month; however, for the last 6 months, this has fallen steadily and last month there were only 5 referrals to him.  John was unsure as to why referrals were falling off and had blamed it on the economic downturn until one of his friends in another firm of solicitors (who did not know John was a shareholder in the Company) mentioned that he and the Company were cross-referring clients very successfully. John was very annoyed and confronted Bob, Patrick and Patty, claiming that in ceasing to maintain his level of referrals they were acting in disregard of his interests as a shareholder.  He also pointed to the below market rent he charged the Company for its premises. John also objected to not being told about their decision to make referrals elsewhere. Their response was that they were acting in the interests of the Company and had never asked for a lower rent and that he was welcome to seek to review the rent if he thought it was not at market value. They also indicated that if this was John’s attitude, they would not make any referrals to him, a statement which provoked John to claim they were acting oppressively. You are required to advise John as to the following matters: Whether John has a remedy under section 205 of the Companies Act 1963; and Whether your answer would be different if the articles of association or a shareholders’ agreement contained a provision that the Company would support shareholders’ business by referring clients to them. In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.  You must distinguish your answers to parts (a) and (b) of the question, both of which carry equal marks.

questions.description: Sarah and Andrew are directors of Famous Fillings Limited (the “Company”) which is in the business of supplying sandwich…
questions.text: Sarah and Andrew are directors of Famous Fillings Limited (the “Company”) which is in the business of supplying sandwich fillings to coffee shops. The Company’s business has held up reasonably well throughout the recession and it has been solvent. Some months ago one of the shops to which it supplies sandwich filling (Sean’s Shop) complained that one of its customers who had bought a sandwich was suing the shop because the customer claimed he had found a mouse’s tail in a tuna sandwich, which he had half eaten and which made him extremely ill. On receiving a solicitor’s letter from the customer, Sean’s Shop agreed to pay him €15,000 in compensation. This was accepted by the customer who had recently been made unemployed and Sean’s Shop demanded that the Company reimburse the €15,000 compensation which it had paid. Sarah and Andrew have never before had such a claim and were shocked at the allegation that a mouse’s tail could have found its way into their tuna mix. The area where the sandwich filling is prepared and packed is a sealed area and they are absolutely confident that the tail could not have found its way into the food before it left their premises, and so they have denied the claim and have refused to reimburse the shop. Sean’s Shop wrote to the Company demanding that it pay the €15,000. When this was ignored Sean’s Shop posted a demand and expressed to be pursuant to Section 214(a) of the Companies Act 1963 to the Company. Four weeks later the Company receives a petition from Sean’s Shop to have the Company wound up on the grounds that it is unable to pay its debts and advising that the petition will be advertised in two newspapers in two days time. You are required to advise Sarah and Andrew.  In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the “Company”) which has been in the busine…
questions.text: Richard and Frank are equal shareholders and directors in RF Events Limited (the “Company”) which has been in the business of organising corporate events since 2005. Business was very strong for the last number of years and whilst the Company has a core staff of 12 employees, at times it employees, at times it employed up to 30 people. In the last year, business has never been worse. Clients are either not hosting events or cancelling events already planned. Richard and Frank have cut staff salaries by 20% and have stopped taking a salary for themselves. Even so, the Company is struggling to keep afloat and is behind on the rent for the premises and its bank loan of some €2,500,000 secured by a mortgage debenture, incorporating a floating charge and a fixed charge on the Company’s premises, which is now worth only €1,750,000. This month it is unlikely to be able to pay to the revenue commissioners the full amount of VAT it collected. Richard and Frank believe that if the Company can only keep afloat for the next 6 months, it stands a good chance of surviving as they believe the worst of the recession is over. Frank has approached an old business colleague, now retired, and asked him to become an investor in the company offering him 25% of the Company (12 ½ % each from him and Richard). Matters came to a head yesterday when the Company’s bank appointed a receiver on account of the Company’s bank loan being in arrears. Richard and Frank approach you for advice on: the steps involved in bringing a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; the likelihood of success of a petition to have an examiner appointed to the Company; and the consequence for the Company and its creditors of an examiner being appointed. You are required to advise Richard and Frank in relation to the matters set out at (a), (b) and (c) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. You are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.text: “Section 205 of the Companies Acts 1963 provides minority shareholders in an Irish company with a significant remedy where they are being oppressed or their interests are being disregarded. There are, however, a number of limitations on the availability of section 205 and on the remedies which it empowers the Court to grant.” You are required to discuss the limitations to section 205 as described in the forgoing statement, citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of DRK Limited (the “Company”), the other director of which is h…
questions.text: Damien is the sole shareholder and one of two directors of DRK Limited (the “Company”), the other director of which is his wife, Deirdre. The Company operated the business of travel agent for a number of years. The Company has loan facilities from Big Bank Plc which are secured by a floating charge over the Company’s assets, a fixed charge over its leasehold interest in the premises it rents and the personal guarantees of its directors, Damien and Deirdre. The six months ending 31 December 2009 were the worst ever experienced by the Company which made nearly all of its employees redundant. Wages were owed to some of the employees and one of them, Sean, was constantly calling to Damien’s and Deirdre’s family home seeking payment. In early January 2010, the revenue commissioners petitioned to have the company wound up on the grounds that it was unable to pay its debts and the High Court granted that petition, appointing Jack Harmer the official liquidator. You are retained by Jack to advise as to the law relating to the following payments by the Company: Christmas week, when Sean called at the directors’ home seeking payment, Damien was out and Deirdre, who was concerned at Sean’s ever-threatening manner, wrote him a cheque for €5000 in respect of wages owed to him; The morning that the petition was heard by the High Court, Damien transferred €30, 000 from one account the Company had into its overdrawn current account; The day after the petition was presented, the Company’s bank debited its current account for €10, 000 in respect of accrued interest on the Company’s loans; A month after the petition to wind up the Company, the liquidator himself made a payment from the Company’s overdrawn account to the Company’s landlord in respect of the premises it occupied as otherwise its lease would have been forfeited. You are required to advise Jack the liquidator on the law in relation to each of the four payments, distinguishing (a), (b), (c) and (d) in your answer. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: In November of 2009 James acquired all of the shares in a single-member private limited company called ‘Dream On This Li…
questions.text: In November of 2009 James acquired all of the shares in a single-member private limited company called ‘Dream On This Limited’ (the “Company”) which operated a retail business selling beds and mattresses. Prior to this the shares in the Company had been held by Jon who together with Aida were its only two directors. The deal to buy the shares in the company had been concluded quickly for €2,000,000 a sum which James considered to be at the lower end of the Company’s value. Because he considered he was getting a good deal, James did not conduct a full due diligence of the Company and the sale was completed by stock transfer form alone without any share purchase agreement. Jon and Aida also resigned as directors of the Company and were replaced by James and his wife Jenny. In February of 2010 James approaches you for advice. He believes that he has discovered a number of irregularities in the conduct of the Company’s affairs, drawing the following matters to your attention: A contract which the Company had with the HSE for the supply of mattresses to the health service was assigned by the Company to a different company owned by Aida in August of 2009. In consequence, the Company’s profitability has decreased significantly having lost one of its largest customers. In March 2009 a loan of €150,000 was made by the Company to Jon which sum remains outstanding. On contacting Jon, James was told that it had been agreed with the Company at the time the loan was made that it would not be repaid for 5 years and that Jon was holding the Company to this arrangement. You are required to advise James on the liabilities of the former directors in respect of the matters detailed in paragraphs (a) and (b) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. In your answer you must cote all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Last year Blue Chip Properties Limited (the “Company”) were placed into creditors’ voluntary liquidation on the basis it…
questions.text: Last year Blue Chip Properties Limited (the “Company”) were placed into creditors’ voluntary liquidation on the basis it was insolvent owing considerable sums to its bank, trade creditors and the revenue commissioners. The Company had acquired a portfolio of 20 apartments and town houses in Cork city which it let to its tenants. In the good times, after servicing the Company’s borrowings, the rent flow from the properties produced a net revenue stream of about €100,000 per annum. The collapse in rental incomes, increased taxation, the collapse of property prices, and most recently flooding, brought the Company to its knees, and the decision was taken to place it into liquidation when it fell behind in VAT payments to the revenue commissioners, and payments to its trade creditors and bank. The Company’s borrowings were provided by Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) which had given it a series of term loans to acquire 20 properties. The Bank’s security was a mortgage debenture comprising a floating charge, and also twenty fixed mortgages and charges over the 20 properties. The Bank also had a charge over an account into which the rent was paid in respect of the five most recently acquired investment properties, and the Company was unable to withdraw funds from that account without the consent of the Bank. The Bank has also appointed a receiver to the Company on foot of the mortgage debenture comprising the floating charge, and the receiver is collecting rents and using the proceeds to repay the Bank. Following his appointment, the Company’s liquidator inspected the security which the Bank holds for its loans to the Company. The liquidator tells you that: through an oversight, the mortgage debenture in favour of the Bank comprising the floating charge which contained the power to appoint the receiver was not registered in the Companies Registration Office under Section 99 of the Companies Act 1963; and the charge over the account into which the rent was paid was registered in the Companies Registration Office as a fixed charge over the Company’s book debts but the revenue commissioners are claiming priority of the amounts due to them ahead of sums due to the bank. You are required to advise the liquidator on the enforceability of the security described in (a) and (b) which should be clearly distinguished in your answer. In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the “Company”) in which they are equal 50:50 shareholders. The C…
questions.text: Leonard and Larry are directors of LL Builders Limited (the “Company”) in which they are equal 50:50 shareholders. The Company’s business has been the purchase, development and resale of period houses. The Company has operated very successfully for the last 10 years; Leonard taking responsibility for the purchase and sale of the properties and Larry looking after the development and building works. Over the last five years Leonard had told Larry that he had put together a portfolio of properties for the Company and that he had been purchasing suitable properties as they came to market so as to ensure a steady supply of properties was available for development by Larry. Larry comes to you for advice. He tells you that he has discovered that Leonard had been acquiring properties not only for the Company but for another company owned by Leonard’s son, Frederick. Larry suspects that the better properties (acquired more cheaply with greatest potential for a higher resale value) were siphoned off to Frederick’s company.  Larry tells you that the Company has three properties on its books now which are worth about 25% less than what the Company paid for them a couple of years ago.  Larry believes he has identified two other properties which were destined to be acquired by the Company but instead were diverted to Frederick’s company. However, Larry has no reason to suspect that these properties too are now worth less than Frederick paid for them.  Larry also tells you that he believes that one derelict property which was acquired by the Company was subsequently sold to Frederick’s company for the same price as it paid for it.  Larry suspects this property is now worth less than the Company paid for it. You are required to advise Larry of his remedies and those of the Company against Leonard. In your answer you are required to refer to all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “Company”), which …
questions.text:
David, Deborah and Cindy are equal shareholders, holding 50 shares each in Foxtrot Tango Limited (the “Company”), which was established by their late mother Madge, having acquired their shares under her will. The Company is in the business of retailing carpet and its only two directors are David and Deborah who also work in the Company. Cindy left the country a number of years ago and lives with her husband in San Francisco and over the years has not had any involvement in the Company.  Once a year she contacts her brother and sister to ask how the Company is doing and usually, following that call, David and Deborah declare a dividend which the Company pays to Cindy, averaging over €50,000 per annum for the last three years. Last Christmas when Cindy was home in Ireland she tentatively enquired from David and Deborah whether they would be interested in buying her shares in the Company.  They said they would have to think about it because it was hard to know what the shares were worth, given the economic downturn. David and Deborah come to you for advice when Cindy contacted them again and announced that she has sold her shares in the Company to Joe, a local businessman in the same town as where the Company has its premises.  A couple of days later David and Deborah were approached by Joe who gave them a stock transfer signed by Cindy and asked them to register him as a member. David and Deborah are unhappy to share the family business with an outsider and ask you for your advice.  On examination of the Company’s articles of association, you see that Regulation 3 of Part II of Table A applies: “The directors may, in their absolute discretion, and without assigning any reason therefore, decline to register any transfer of any share, whether or not it is a fully paid share”.
You are required to advise David and Deborah on their rights and obligations in relation to the transfer of shares to Joe.  In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Niamh and Ruth are directors of and equal 50:50 shareholders in Story Book Sales Limited (the “Company”) which is in the…
questions.text: Niamh and Ruth are directors of and equal 50:50 shareholders in Story Book Sales Limited (the “Company”) which is in the business of visiting offices and leaving samples of books with the people, and then coming back to take their orders to buy the books.  Business has been reasonably good over the years, but recently sales have not been as good. Niamh is shocked to be told by Ruth that they have not paid a number of their creditors for some time. The revenue commissioners have not been paid VAT for two months, their suppliers of the books have also not been paid, and whilst they have continued to collect money from the people in the offices they may not be able to get the books they ordered because their supplier has refused to provide any more books to meet the orders.  When Niamh asks to see the books of the company Ruth confesses that she has not been keeping proper books and that the Company’s records are “a bit of a mess”.  Not only have annual returns not been made to the Companies Registration Office, but many of the Company’s records and receipts are usually kept by Ruth in the book of her car! Realising that the company is insolvent, and at Niamh’s urging, Ruth eventually agrees to put the Company into creditors’ voluntary liquidation and a liquidator is appointed. Niamh approaches you for advice on the likelihood that she will be restricted pursuant to section 150 of the Companies Act 1990.  You are required to advise Niamh on the law relating to the restriction of directors and to cite all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.text: “The floating charge, a securing unique to companies, has a number of key characteristics which distinguish it from a fixed charge.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Trader-Upper Brokers Limited (the “Company”) is a mortgage broker company of which Fred and Richard are the only two dir…
questions.text: Trader-Upper Brokers Limited (the “Company”) is a mortgage broker company of which Fred and Richard are the only two directors and equal shareholders.  The Company is finding the going tough as it specialised in securing mortgage finance from banks and building societies for upwardly-mobile people interested in “trading-up” their homes to bigger and better residences.  The Company’s finances have been severely affected by the recession: finance has not been available to people to buy houses; houses are not selling and where the Company introduced business to the banks and building societies their commission was less than it had previously been. Frank and Richard have not reduced their salaries of €15,000 per month even though the company’s income has fallen dramatically.  They continue to incur debts to other creditors and to trade.  They approach you for advice when their auditor warned them that to continue as they are could result in their being found to have traded fraudulently or recklessly. You are required to advise Frank and Richard on the law relating to reckless trading and fraudulent trading.  In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Ruby runs a wine and port distribution business in Dublin.  He comes to you for advice telling you that he is owed some …
questions.text: Ruby runs a wine and port distribution business in Dublin.  He comes to you for advice telling you that he is owed some €50,000 from a company called Ever Optimistic Builders Limited, in respect of drink supplied to it before Christmas for which Ruby has not been paid.  Ruby tells you that all contact with Ever Optimistic Builders Limited has been futile and it is clear to him that the company has no intention of paying him the money it owes.  Ruby tells you that this company has been slow in paying him on a number of occasions in the past and that it was against his better judgment to supply the wine before Christmas as he dislikes and distrusts Fred, the managing director of the company.  Ruby acknowledges to you that he owes Ever Optimistic Builders Limited €30,000 in respect of an extension which was carried out to his home but that he has no intention of paying that until his bill is first discharged. Ruby asks you to explain the steps involved in bringing a petition to wind up Ever Optimistic Builders Limited. You are required to advise Ruby on the law relating to presenting a petition to have a company wound up on the grounds that it is unable to pay its debts.  In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law and attempt to apply these to the facts provided above.

questions.description: Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) is your client and seeks your advice in relation to security it proposes to take in support of…
questions.text: Big Bank plc (the “Bank”) is your client and seeks your advice in relation to security it proposes to take in support of a loan to a company called Can-Do Limited (the “Company”).  The Company is seeking to borrow €5,000,000 to finance construction works it has been contracted to carry out.  The security which has been offered is as follows: (a) a first fixed charge over the Company’s book debts due under the contract and over a bank account into which the Company proposes to pay 75% of each of the five €1m instalments due on foot of the contract; and (b) a floating charge over its plants and machinery. You are required to advise the Bank on the law applicable to each of the two proposed securities so as to ensure that they will be valid and enforceable.  In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the “Company”) and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given…
questions.text: Charlie is a director of Rainy Days Limited (the “Company”) and holds 10% of its issued share capital. Charlie was given his 10% of the shares in the Company by his two fellow shareholding directors as a reward for his many years of service with the Company. The Company is in the printing business and Charlie is the chief typesetter who looks after the printing.  Charlie’s fellow directors, Richard and Martin, hold between them the remaining 90% of the issued shares in the Company. At the prompting of his brother-in-law, in whom Charlie has been confiding, Charlie comes to you for advice after learning that his fellow directors have recently transferred a significant asset out of the company for what seems to be its market value. Charlie tells you that the Company never really had formal board meetings and that things were no different on this occasion, Richard and Martin simply taking it upon themselves to give effect to the transfer.  Charlie has explained that any time he has objected to this course of action, Richard and Martin have reminded him that once they agree to a course of action, there is little point in having a board meeting since the board acts by majority and he would be outvoted; they also remind him that as they hold 90% of the shares they can remove him as a director if they see ft so he has little reason for complaint. You are required to advise Charlie on the law relating to minority shareholder oppression and the remedies available to a member of a company and say whether in your opinion Charlie has a case against the Company or its directors.  In your answer you are required to cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.description: “It is now well recognised that a company’s members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three way…
questions.text: “It is now well recognised that a company’s members can give their sanction to a course of action in either of three ways: first, by a resolution passed at a general meeting of the company, duly convened and held; secondly, by unanimous written resolution and thirdly, by an estoppel, created through their unanimous informal agreement.” You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.

questions.description: Fast Repairs Limited (the “Company”) is a family company of which Jim and Lucy are its directors.  Jim and Lucy have eac…
questions.text: Fast Repairs Limited (the “Company”) is a family company of which Jim and Lucy are its directors.  Jim and Lucy have each got 20% of the shares in the company as have their three siblings, Lou, Sue and Flo.  Although Lucy is a director, she does not play an active part in the management of the Company and is there to fill the statutory requirement that every company must have two directors.  Jim is the managing director and Lucy has delegated the full power to manage the Company to Jim.  Every year the Company declares a dividend for its five shareholders and in addition to his share of the dividend, Jim also received a salary for working in the Company.  Once a year the family get together for a briefing on the Company given by Jim and at that meeting the accounts for the previous year are signed and the annual return completed. The Company’s business is that of shoe repairs and business has never been better.  Turnover has doubled in the last 12 months and three additional employees have had to be taken on to cater for demand.  New premises are required and Jim proposes to rent an adjacent premises (which he owns personally) to the Company to enable it to expand its business; Jim is proposing a lump sum of €25,000 in key money to be paid to him and an annual rent of €30,000 thereafter which Jim believes reflects the market value of his premises.  Jim also proposes making a loan to the Company to enable it to fund the key money and intends to charge the Company an interest rate of 5% which, again, Jim believes to be reasonable. Jim is a conscientious sort and approaches you for advice on the formalities which he should observe in putting these transactions into effect.  You are required to advise Jim in relation to the duties owed by a director in these circumstances and as to how Jim should go about discharging those duties.  In your answer you must cite all relevant case and statute law.

questions.text: “Corporate capacity may be classified as comprised of substantive objects, express ancillary powers or implied ancillary powers”. You are required to discuss this statement citing all relevant case and statute law in your answer.
